questions.specification:
Ammonia is formed by the Haber process:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$$

The diagram below shows the energy profile of this reaction.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/P9PZaDQQyllPzP-YJOVS8.jpeg)

parts.content: State what is meant by the term activation energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Define activation energy as the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understanding Collision Theory
steps.content:
To understand how ammonia is formed in the Haber process, we look at **Collision Theory** (covered in **Topic R2.2**). This theory states that for a reaction to occur, reactant particles (like $N_2$ and $H_2$) must collide with each other. 

However, a simple collision isn't enough; the particles must collide with sufficient energy to break the existing chemical bonds in the reactants so that new ones can form.

steps.title: Defining Activation Energy
steps.content:
In the context of collision theory, **activation energy** ($E_a$) is the specific "energy barrier" that must be overcome. 

It is defined as the **minimum energy** that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. Without this minimum amount of kinetic energy, the particles will simply bounce off one another without reacting.

steps.title: Final Answer Formulation
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you should focus on the "minimum" requirement. 

**Activation energy:** The minimum amount of energy required for a reaction to occur upon collision.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: In the provided energy profile diagram, the reaction starts with the reactants ($N_2(g) + 3H_2(g)$) at a specific potential energy level. To progress toward the products, the system must first reach the highest point of the curve, known as the **transition state** or the peak of the energy barrier.

steps.title: Identify activation energy
steps.content:
The activation energy ($E_a$) is represented on the diagram as the energy difference between the **reactants** and the **transition state**. 

It is the "energy hill" that the reactants must climb before they can be converted into products. 

$$\text{Activation Energy} = \text{Energy of Transition State} - \text{Energy of Reactants}$$

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you should define it as the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. This is the energy needed to break the bonds in the reactants so that new bonds can form.

This concept is a key part of **Collision Theory** (Topic R2.2), which explains that only collisions with energy equal to or greater than the $E_a$ will result in a successful reaction.

highlights.text: minimum energy required for a reaction to occur

parts.content: Identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic and justify your answer using the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the reaction is exothermic. <marks>A1</marks>
- Justify that the products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating that energy is released to the surroundings. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the energy profile diagram. The horizontal line on the left represents the potential energy of the **reactants** ($N_2 + 3H_2$), and the horizontal line on the right represents the potential energy of the **products** ($2NH_3$).

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in the IB syllabus.

steps.title: Compare potential energy levels
steps.content:
By observing the vertical axis (Potential Energy), we can see that the line for the products is physically lower than the line for the reactants. 

This means that the products have less stored chemical energy than the starting materials.

highlights.text: Identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic and justify your answer using the diagram.

steps.title: Classify the reaction
steps.content:
Since the products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, the "missing" energy must have been released to the surroundings, usually as heat. 

According to the markscheme, we identify this as an **exothermic** reaction. 

**Justification:** The products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating that energy is released to the surroundings.

steps.title: Compare energy levels
steps.content: To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the relative energy levels of the reactants and products on the energy profile diagram. We can see that the products ($2NH_3$) are at a lower energy level than the reactants ($N_2 + 3H_2$). This indicates a "downward step" in energy as the reaction proceeds from start to finish.

steps.title: Determine enthalpy change sign
steps.content:
The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is defined as the difference between the energy of the products and the energy of the reactants. Based on the data booklet formula for enthalpy change:

$$\Delta H^{\ominus}=\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products})-\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

Since the products have less energy than the reactants, the value of $\Delta H$ will be negative ($\Delta H < 0$). For example, if we represent the product energy as $2$ and reactant energy as $10$, the change is $2 - 10 = -8$.

steps.title: Classify as exothermic
steps.content: Because the enthalpy change is negative, we conclude that the reaction is **exothermic**. This means that energy is released from the system into the surroundings as the chemical bonds in the products are formed. This concept is covered in **Reactivity 1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

highlights.text: Identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic

parts.content: Suggest one reason why a high temperature is required for this reaction to proceed at a practical rate.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that because the reaction has a high activation energy, a high temperature is needed so that more particles have energy $E \ge E_a$ to react upon collision. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understand temperature and energy
steps.content:
In chemistry, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. When we increase the temperature of the reactants (nitrogen and hydrogen), we are giving the particles more energy and causing them to move faster. 

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content: Looking at the energy profile diagram, there is a significant energy barrier that must be overcome for the reactants to turn into products. This is known as the **activation energy** ($E_a$). For a collision between molecules to result in a reaction, the colliding particles must possess energy equal to or greater than this value ($E \ge E_a$).

steps.title: Relate temperature to collisions
steps.content:
The Haber process has a relatively high activation energy. At low temperatures, very few particles have enough energy to overcome this barrier. By using a high temperature, we increase the average kinetic energy of the system, which significantly **increases the proportion of particles** that have energy $E \ge E_a$.

Because more particles meet the energy requirement, the frequency of **successful (effective) collisions** increases, allowing the reaction to proceed at a practical rate for industrial use.

steps.title: Identify the Arrhenius Equation
steps.content:
To understand why temperature affects the reaction rate, we look at the **Arrhenius equation** from the IB Data Booklet. This equation shows the relationship between the rate constant ($k$), the activation energy ($E_a$), and the temperature ($T$):

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

Where:
- $k$ is the rate constant (directly proportional to the reaction rate).
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $R$ is the gas constant.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

steps.title: Analyze the Energy Profile
steps.content:
Looking at the provided energy profile, we can see a significant energy barrier between the reactants ($N_2 + 3H_2$) and the products. This hump represents the activation energy ($E_a$). 

In the Haber process, the activation energy is high because a large amount of energy is required to break the strong triple bond in the $N_2$ molecule ($N \equiv N$).

steps.title: Evaluate the Exponential Term
steps.content:
In the equation $$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$, the term $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ represents the fraction of collisions with enough energy to overcome the activation barrier. 

Because $E_a$ is in the numerator of a negative exponent, a **high activation energy** makes the term $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ very small. If the temperature ($T$) is low, the rate constant $k$ becomes so small that the reaction is too slow to be useful.

steps.title: Justify High Temperature
steps.content:
To achieve a "practical rate," we need a larger value for $k$. By increasing the temperature ($T$):
1. The value of the fraction $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ decreases.
2. This makes the negative exponent $\frac{-E_a}{RT}$ less negative (closer to zero).
3. Consequently, the value of $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ increases significantly, leading to a much larger rate constant $k$.

Therefore, a high temperature is required to ensure that a sufficient fraction of particles have energy $E \ge E_a$ to react upon collision.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the exponential part of the Arrhenius equation for two different temperatures.

desmos.instructions: Plot the function y = e^(-100000/(8.31*x)) to see how the rate constant factor increases with temperature x.

calculator.description: Visualize how increasing T increases k. If Ea = 100,000 J/mol and R = 8.31, compare k (proportional to the exponential term) at 300K vs 700K.

parts.content: Explain how the use of a catalyst would affect the energy profile diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- State that a catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe that on the diagram, the peak of the energy barrier would be lower. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the catalyst's role
steps.content:
In chemistry, a catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway** (or mechanism) for the reactants to turn into products. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**. 

Instead of the original high-energy route, the catalyst allows the molecules to react via a different series of steps that is more energetically favorable.

steps.title: Lowering the activation energy
steps.content:
Because this alternative pathway is more efficient, it has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$). 

Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. By lowering this requirement, a greater proportion of particles will have enough energy to react at any given temperature, which increases the reaction rate.

highlights.text: State that a catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.

steps.title: Effect on the diagram
steps.content:
On an energy profile diagram, the activation energy is represented by the height of the curve from the reactants to the top of the curve. 

When a catalyst is used, the **peak of the energy barrier** (the maximum point on the curve) is **lower** than the original peak. It is important to note that the energy levels of the reactants and products remain exactly the same; only the "hill" in between them gets smaller.

highlights.text: Describe that on the diagram, the peak of the energy barrier would be lower.

parts.content: The Haber process is a reversible reaction. State and explain one condition used in industry to increase the yield of ammonia.
parts.markscheme:
- State that high pressure is used. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that there are fewer moles of gas on the product side (4 moles of reactants $\to$ 2 moles of products), so high pressure shifts the equilibrium position toward the products according to Le Chatelier's principle. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State the industrial condition
steps.content: To increase the yield of ammonia in the Haber process, the industrial condition used is **high pressure** (typically around 200 atmospheres).

highlights.text: State that high pressure is used.

steps.title: Analyze gaseous stoichiometry
steps.content:
We first look at the coefficients of the gaseous species in the balanced equation:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$$

- On the **reactant side**, there are $1 + 3 = 4$ moles of gas.
- On the **product side**, there are $2$ moles of gas.

Since 4 is greater than 2, the product side has a lower total number of gas molecules.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle**, if the pressure of a system at equilibrium is increased, the system will shift to oppose that change by favoring the side with **fewer moles of gas**.

Because the production of ammonia results in fewer moles of gas (2 moles of $NH_3$ vs 4 moles of reactants), increasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium position to the **right** (the product side). This shift increases the percentage yield of ammonia.

highlights.text: Explain that there are fewer moles of gas on the product side (4 moles of reactants $\to$ 2 moles of products), so high pressure shifts the equilibrium position toward the products according to Le Chatelier's principle.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
First, let's look at the provided energy profile. We can see that the energy level of the products ($2NH_3$) is lower than the energy level of the reactants ($N_2 + 3H_2$). 

This tells us that energy is released during the reaction, meaning the forward reaction is **exothermic** ($ΔH$ is negative). You can review this concept in **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

steps.title: State the condition
steps.content: To increase the yield of ammonia ($NH_3$) based on temperature, the condition used is a **lower temperature**.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle** (Topic R2.3.4), if we decrease the temperature, the system will shift its equilibrium position to counteract that change by producing more heat.

Since the forward reaction is exothermic, it releases heat:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g) + \text{heat}$$

Decreasing the temperature causes the equilibrium to shift to the **right** (the product side) to generate more heat, thereby increasing the yield of ammonia.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
In an exam, your two marks would be awarded as follows:

1.  **State the condition:** Use a low(er) temperature. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Explain the shift:** The forward reaction is exothermic, so a decrease in temperature shifts the equilibrium to the right to favor the production of ammonia. <marks>R1</marks>

*Note: While low temperature increases yield, industry actually uses a "compromise" temperature (around $450^{\circ}C$) to ensure the reaction happens fast enough to be profitable!*

steps.title: Identify the industrial condition
steps.content:
To increase the yield of ammonia in the Haber process, one common industrial condition is the **continuous removal of the product**, ammonia ($NH_3$), from the equilibrium mixture as it is formed.

In practice, this is achieved by cooling the gases so that the ammonia liquefies and can be tapped off, while the unreacted nitrogen and hydrogen remain as gases and are recycled back into the reactor.

highlights.text: State and explain one condition used in industry to increase the yield of ammonia.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's principle**, if a system at equilibrium is disturbed by a change in conditions, the system will shift its equilibrium position to oppose the change.

By removing $NH_3$, we are decreasing the concentration of the product. The system opposes this decrease by favoring the forward reaction to produce more $NH_3$. This shifts the equilibrium position toward the **right (product side)**.

steps.title: Summarize the effect
steps.content:
Because the ammonia is removed continuously, the system never actually reaches a final static equilibrium. Instead, it is forced to keep reacting in the forward direction:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow 2NH_3(g)$$

This continuous shift ensures a much higher overall yield of ammonia than would be possible if the products were allowed to remain in the reaction vessel.

questions.specification:
A student burns ethanol in a spirit burner to heat $200\text{ g}$ of water. The water temperature increases by $15.0\,^\circ\text{C}$. What is the heat absorbed by the water?

(Specific heat capacity of water, $c = 4.18\text{ J}\,\text{g}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}$)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Calculating heat absorbed by water from mass, temperature change, and specific heat capacity (q = mcΔT) is an energy-transfer/enthalpy measurement skill in calorimetry.

options.markscheme: Incorrect calculation. This value might be obtained by using an incorrect temperature change or mass in the calculation.

The heat absorbed is calculated using the calorimetry equation:
$q = mc\Delta T$
$q = 200\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J}\,\text{g}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1} \times 15.0\text{ K} = 12540\text{ J}$
Incorrect calculation. This value would be obtained if the mass of water used was $50\text{ g}$ instead of $200\text{ g}$ ($50 \times 4.18 \times 15.0 = 3135$).
Incorrect calculation. This value would be obtained if the temperature change was mistakenly taken as $20.0\text{ K}$ instead of $15.0\text{ K}$ ($200 \times 4.18 \times 20.0 = 16720$).

Endothermic reactions have a positive $\Delta H$ value.
A negative $\Delta H$ indicates that the system has lost enthalpy to the surroundings, meaning the reaction is exothermic and releases heat.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the enthalpy change when potassium chloride ($\mathrm{KCl}$) dissolves in water.
They dissolve $2.00\ \mathrm{g}$ of $\mathrm{KCl(s)}$ in $100.0\ \mathrm{g}$ of water and observe the temperature drop from $25.00^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $22.50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.
The specific heat capacity of water is $4.18\ \mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ {}^{\circ}C^{-1}}$ and the molar mass of $\mathrm{KCl}$ is $74.55\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$.

parts.content: Calculate the heat energy, $Q$, lost by the water using $Q = mc\Delta T$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the temperature difference: $\Delta T = 25.00 - 22.50 = 2.50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
- Substitute into the equation: $Q = 100.0 \times 4.18 \times 2.50$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the final value: $Q = 1045\ \mathrm{J} = 1.05\ \mathrm{kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Calculate temperature change
steps.content:
First, we need to find the change in temperature ($ΔT$) of the water. Since the temperature dropped, we subtract the final temperature from the initial temperature.

$$\Delta T = 25.00^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 22.50^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = 2.50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$$

steps.title: Identify mass and capacity
steps.content:
To use the calorimetry formula, we need the mass of the substance being heated or cooled (in this case, the water) and its specific heat capacity.

From the question:
- Mass ($m$) = $100.0\ \mathrm{g}$
- Specific heat capacity ($c$) = $4.18\ \mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ {}^{\circ}C^{-1}}$

highlights.text: specific heat capacity of water is 4.18\ \mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ {}^{\circ}C^{-1}}

steps.title: Apply calorimetry formula
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values for $m$, $c$, and $\Delta T$ into the heat energy equation provided in the question.

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$
$$Q = 100.0 \times 4.18 \times 2.50$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 100.0 * 4.18 * 2.50 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, type 100.0 * 4.18 * 2.50 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total heat energy in Joules.

highlights.text: Calculate the heat energy, Q, lost by the water using Q = mc\Delta T

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the values gives $Q = 1045\ \mathrm{J}$. 

In Chemistry, we often express energy in kilojoules (kJ) for convenience. To convert, we divide by $1000$:

$$Q = \frac{1045}{1000} = 1.045\ \mathrm{kJ}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching our temperature change and heat capacity), we get:

$$Q = 1.05\ \mathrm{kJ}$$

parts.content: Calculate the amount of $\mathrm{KCl}$ dissolved (in mol).
parts.markscheme: - Calculate moles: $n = \dfrac{2.00}{74.55} = 0.0268\ \mathrm{mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the amount of substance in moles, we first need to identify the mass and the molar mass of potassium chloride ($\text{KCl}$) provided in the question.

- Mass of $\text{KCl}$ ($m$) = $2.00\ \text{g}$
- Molar mass of $\text{KCl}$ ($M$) = $74.55\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
In chemistry, we relate mass to the amount in moles using the molar mass formula found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet.

$$\text{amount (mol)} = \frac{\text{mass (g)}}{\text{molar mass (g mol}^{-1}\text{)}}$$

Symbolically, this is written as:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

steps.title: Calculate the moles
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{2.00\ \text{g}}{74.55\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} \\ n &\approx 0.0268\ \text{mol} \end{aligned}$$

We round the final answer to **three significant figures** to match the precision of the mass given in the problem ($2.00\ \text{g}$).

steps.title: Calculator Instructions
steps.content: You can quickly perform this division on any graphing calculator to ensure accuracy.

gdc.instructions: Enter 2.00 / 74.55 in the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing mass by molar mass.

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To find the amount of $\mathrm{KCl}$ in moles, we first identify the mass of the sample and the molar mass of the compound from the question.

- Mass of $\mathrm{KCl} (m) = 2.00\ \mathrm{g}$
- Molar mass of $\mathrm{KCl} (M) = 74.55\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
In dimensional analysis, we treat the molar mass as a conversion factor to switch between units. Since we are starting with grams and want to find moles, we use the factor:

$$\frac{1\ \mathrm{mol}}{74.55\ \mathrm{g}}$$

This follows the general relationship for moles found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

steps.title: Calculate the amount
steps.content:
Now, multiply the given mass by the conversion factor. Notice how the gram units cancel out, leaving us with moles:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= 2.00\ \mathrm{g} \times \frac{1\ \mathrm{mol}}{74.55\ \mathrm{g}} \\ n &= \frac{2.00}{74.55}\ \mathrm{mol} \\ n &\approx 0.0268\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the mass $2.00\ \mathrm{g}$), we get **$0.0268\ \mathrm{mol}$**.

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, divide 2.00 by 74.55.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.00 divided by 74.55 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing mass by molar mass.

parts.content: Determine the enthalpy change of solution for $\mathrm{KCl}$ in $\mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$. Include the sign.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values: $\Delta H = \dfrac{+1.05\ \mathrm{kJ}}{0.0268\ \mathrm{mol}}$
- Calculate final value: $+39.2\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct positive sign <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Calculate heat energy change
steps.content:
First, we calculate the heat energy change ($q$) for the surrounding water using the formula $q = mc\Delta T$, where $m$ is the mass of the water, $c$ is the specific heat capacity, and $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature.

The temperature drop is:
$$\Delta T = 25.00^{\circ}\text{C} - 22.50^{\circ}\text{C} = 2.50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Now, calculate $q$ in Joules:
$$q = 100.0\ \text{g} \times 4.18\ \text{J g}^{-1}\ {}^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1} \times 2.50^{\circ}\text{C} = 1045\ \text{J}$$

Convert this to kilojoules (kJ):
$$q = \frac{1045}{1000} = 1.045\ \text{kJ}$$

Based on the markscheme, we can round this to $1.05\ \text{kJ}$ for our final calculation.

ti84.instructions: Enter 100.0 * 4.18 * 2.50 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the heat energy change (q).

steps.title: Calculate moles of solute
steps.content:
Next, we find the number of moles ($n$) of the solute, $\text{KCl}$, that was dissolved. We use the formula for moles from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substituting the given mass ($2.00\ \text{g}$) and molar mass ($74.55\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$):

$$n = \frac{2.00\ \text{g}}{74.55\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0268\ \text{mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.00 / 74.55 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of KCl.

steps.title: Determine enthalpy and sign
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the enthalpy change of solution ($\Delta H_{\text{sol}}$) by dividing the heat energy by the number of moles:

$$\Delta H_{\text{sol}} = \frac{q}{n}$$

Using the values from the previous steps:
$$\Delta H_{\text{sol}} = \frac{+1.05\ \text{kJ}}{0.0268\ \text{mol}} = +39.2\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Determining the sign:** The temperature of the water **dropped** from $25.00^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $22.50^{\circ}\text{C}$. This means the reaction absorbed heat from the surroundings, making it an **endothermic** process. In IB Chemistry, endothermic reactions always have a **positive (+)** sign.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.05 / 0.0268 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final enthalpy change.

steps.title: Calculate temperature change
steps.content:
First, we determine the change in temperature ($\Delta T$) for the water. Since the temperature dropped, we calculate the magnitude of this change:

$$\Delta T = 25.00^{\circ}\text{C} - 22.50^{\circ}\text{C} = 2.50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

steps.title: Calculate heat for sample
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the heat energy ($q$) absorbed by the dissolution of the $2.00\text{ g}$ sample. We use the specific heat capacity formula, focusing on the mass of the water where the heat transfer occurs:

$$\begin{aligned} q &= m \times c \times \Delta T \\ q &= 100.0\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1\ \circ}\text{C}^{-1} \times 2.50^{\circ}\text{C} \\ q &= 1045\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Converting this to kilojoules:

$$q = 1.045\text{ kJ}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the energy.

calculator.description: Calculate the heat energy in Joules.

steps.title: Scale to one mole
steps.content:
Using the **Proportional Scaling** approach, we compare the mass of our sample ($2.00\text{ g}$) to the mass of one mole ($74.55\text{ g}$). 

We calculate the energy for one mole by scaling the heat of our sample by the ratio of the molar mass to the sample mass:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{\text{sol}} &= q_{\text{sample}} \times \frac{\text{Molar Mass}}{\text{Sample Mass}} \\ \Delta H_{\text{sol}} &= 1.045\text{ kJ} \times \frac{74.55\text{ g mol}^{-1}}{2.00\text{ g}} \\ \Delta H_{\text{sol}} &\approx 38.95\text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Based on the markscheme's rounding of intermediate values ($1.05\text{ kJ}$ and $0.0268\text{ mol}$), the final value is accepted as $+39.2\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the heat by the ratio of molar mass to sample mass.

calculator.description: Scale the heat to find the enthalpy per mole.

highlights.text: molar mass of KCl is 74.55 g mol^-1

steps.title: Determine final sign
steps.content:
Since the temperature of the water **dropped**, the reaction is **endothermic**. This means the system absorbed energy from the surroundings. 

In IB Chemistry, endothermic enthalpy changes must always be assigned a **positive (+)** sign.

Final Answer:
$$\Delta H_{\text{sol}} = +39.2\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate the temperature change
steps.content:
First, we determine the change in temperature ($ΔT$). Since the temperature dropped, the value will be negative when calculated as $T_{final} - T_{initial}$, indicating an endothermic process.

$$\Delta T = 22.50^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 25.00^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = -2.50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$$

For our heat calculation, we use the magnitude of this change, which is $2.50\ \mathrm{K}$ (since a change of $1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is equal to a change of $1\ \mathrm{K}$).

steps.title: Find the total solution mass
steps.content:
In the **Total Solution Mass Method**, we assume that the heat is absorbed from the entire mass of the solution (the solute plus the solvent). 

$$\text{Total mass } (m) = 2.00\ \mathrm{g} \text{ (KCl)} + 100.0\ \mathrm{g} \text{ (water)} = 102.0\ \mathrm{g}$$

steps.title: Calculate heat energy change
steps.content:
Now we calculate the heat energy ($q$) absorbed by the solution using the formula $q = mc\Delta T$. We use the specific heat capacity of water for the whole solution.

$$\begin{aligned} q &= 102.0\ \mathrm{g} \times 4.18\ \mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ K^{-1}} \times 2.50\ \mathrm{K} \\ q &= 1065.9\ \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

Converting to kilojoules:
$$q = 1.0659\ \mathrm{kJ}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication: 102.0 * 4.18 * 2.50

calculator.description: Calculate heat energy in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate moles of KCl
steps.content:
Using the formula from the data booklet, we calculate the number of moles ($n$) of the solute $\mathrm{KCl}$ using its molar mass ($M = 74.55\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$).

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{m}{M} \\ n &= \frac{2.00\ \mathrm{g}}{74.55\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}} \\ n &\approx 0.0268\ \mathrm{mol} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of KCl.

steps.title: Determine enthalpy change
steps.content:
Finally, we find the molar enthalpy change of solution ($\Delta H_{sol}$). 

Since the temperature **decreased**, the reaction is **endothermic**, so $\Delta H$ must have a **positive (+)** sign.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{sol} &= +\frac{q}{n} \\ \Delta H_{sol} &= +\frac{1.0659\ \mathrm{kJ}}{0.0268\ \mathrm{mol}} \\ \Delta H_{sol} &= +39.7\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: In some markschemes, using only the mass of water ($100.0\mathrm{g}$) leads to $+39.2\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$. Both methods are generally understood as valid approaches in enthalpy experiments, but here we used the total solution mass as specified.)*

calculator.description: Divide heat by moles to get the final enthalpy value.

parts.content: State the physical meaning of the sign of your answer to Part 3.
parts.markscheme: - Positive sign indicates that the process absorbs energy from surroundings (endothermic) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the sign
steps.content: In Part 3, we calculated the enthalpy change ($ΔH$) for the dissolution of $\mathrm{KCl}$. Since the temperature of the water decreased, the process resulted in a **positive** value for $\u0394H$.

steps.title: Analyze energy flow
steps.content:
The fact that the temperature of the surroundings (the water) decreased means that heat energy was transferred from the water into the reacting system (the $\mathrm{KCl}$ dissolving). 

A positive enthalpy change ($\u0394H > 0$) indicates that energy is being **absorbed from the surroundings** to break the ionic lattice of $\mathrm{KCl}$.

steps.title: Classify the process
steps.content:
In chemistry, we classify any process that absorbs heat from its surroundings as **endothermic**. 

You can review this classification in **Topic R1.1.2: Endothermic and exothermic reactions**.

Therefore, the positive sign indicates the reaction is endothermic.

highlights.text: Positive sign indicates that the process absorbs energy from surroundings (endothermic)

steps.title: Identify the process type
steps.content:
In the experiment, the student observed a **temperature drop** in the water. This indicates that the dissolving process absorbed thermal energy from the surroundings (the water) into the system (the $\text{KCl}$ solution). This identifies the process as **endothermic**, resulting in a positive enthalpy change ($\Delta H > 0$).

You can review these concepts in **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

steps.title: Compare enthalpy levels
steps.content:
Using the **Comparison of Enthalpy Levels** approach, we interpret the positive sign by looking at the energy of the components involved. The enthalpy change is defined as:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Since $\Delta H$ is positive, it means that the enthalpy of the products (the aqueous ions $\text{K}^{+}\text{(aq)}$ and $\text{Cl}^{-}\text{(aq)}$) is **greater** than the enthalpy of the reactants (the solid salt $\text{KCl(s)}$).

steps.title: State the physical meaning
steps.content: The physical meaning of the positive sign is that there is a **net increase** in the system's internal energy. Because the products are at a higher energy level than the reactants, the system must have **absorbed energy** from the surroundings to facilitate the change.

highlights.text: Positive sign indicates that the process absorbs energy from surroundings (endothermic)

parts.content: Discuss why the dissolution of an ionic solid like $\mathrm{KCl}$ can be spontaneous even when it is endothermic.
parts.markscheme:
- Dissolution increases disorder (entropy of system increases) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S \implies$ large $\Delta S$ can make $\Delta G$ negative, so process is spontaneous <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define spontaneity condition
steps.content: In thermodynamics, a process is considered **spontaneous** if it results in a decrease in Gibbs free energy. This means that for the dissolution of $\mathrm{KCl}$ to occur naturally without external work, the change in Gibbs free energy ($ΔG$) must be negative ($ΔG < 0$).

steps.title: Identify entropy change
steps.content: When solid $\mathrm{KCl}$ dissolves in water, the highly ordered crystal lattice breaks down into individual $\mathrm{K}^+$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ions that move freely throughout the solution. This transition from a solid to an aqueous state significantly increases the **disorder** (randomness) of the system. Therefore, the entropy change ($\Delta S$) for this process is positive ($\Delta S > 0$).

steps.title: Apply Gibbs equation
steps.content:
We use the Gibbs free energy equation from the data booklet to relate these factors:

$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = \Delta H^{\ominus} - T\Delta S^{\ominus}$$

In this experiment, the temperature drop indicates the reaction is **endothermic**, meaning the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is positive. However, because $\Delta S$ is also positive, the term $-T\Delta S$ becomes negative. 

If the magnitude of the entropy term ($T\Delta S$) is larger than the enthalpy term ($\Delta H$), then $\Delta G$ becomes negative, making the dissolution spontaneous.

parts.content: State the meaning of the term endothermic reaction.
parts.markscheme: An endothermic reaction is one that absorbs energy from the surroundings. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define energy transfer
steps.content: In chemistry, we classify reactions based on how energy moves between the **system** (the chemicals) and the **surroundings** (everything else). To answer this, we look at the direction of that energy flow.

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
An **endothermic reaction** is a reaction that results in a net **absorption of energy** (typically heat) from the surroundings into the system. 

This means the system gains energy, which is why the products end up with a higher enthalpy than the reactants.

steps.title: Relate to photosynthesis
steps.content: You can see this in the provided equation where **light energy** is required as an input. Because the system takes in this energy to form products, the energy level of the products is higher than the reactants on the energy profile diagram.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: Looking at the energy profile diagram for photosynthesis, we can see that the energy level of the products (glucose and oxygen) is higher than the energy level of the reactants (carbon dioxide and water). This indicates that energy must be absorbed for the reaction to occur.

steps.title: Define endothermic reaction
steps.content: In an **endothermic reaction**, the system absorbs energy from its surroundings. This is why the equation for photosynthesis includes "light energy" on the reactant side—it is the energy input required to drive the process.

steps.title: Identify thermodynamic change
steps.content:
From a thermodynamic perspective, an endothermic reaction is characterized by a **positive change in enthalpy** ($$\Delta H > 0$$). 

This happens because the enthalpy of the products ($$H_{\text{products}}$$) is greater than the enthalpy of the reactants ($$H_{\text{reactants}}$$). Using the logic from the enthalpy change formula in your data booklet:

$$\Delta H = \sum H_{\text{products}} - \sum H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Since the products are at a higher energy level, the result of this subtraction is a positive value.

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content: To answer the question, an endothermic reaction is one that **absorbs energy from the surroundings**, resulting in a positive enthalpy change ($$\Delta H > 0$$).

steps.title: Analyze the energy diagram
steps.content:
To understand why this reaction is endothermic, let's look at the energy profile diagram. The y-axis represents the potential energy or enthalpy ($H$) of the system. 

Notice the positions of the two horizontal lines:
- The **reactants** ($6\mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) are at a lower energy level.
- The **products** ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6 + 6\mathrm{O}_2$) are at a higher energy level.

This tells us that the enthalpy of the products is greater than the enthalpy of the reactants ($H_{\text{products}} > H_{\text{reactants}}$).

steps.title: Interpret the enthalpy change
steps.content:
In chemistry, the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is calculated as:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Since the products are higher on the graph than the reactants, the value of $\Delta H$ is positive ($+$). For the system to move from a lower energy state to a higher energy state, it must have gained energy from its environment.

steps.title: Define endothermic reaction
steps.content:
Based on our graphical observation that energy is taken in to reach the higher product level, we can state the definition. 

An **endothermic reaction** is a chemical reaction that **absorbs energy (heat) from the surroundings**.

In the context of photosynthesis, this energy is absorbed in the form of light energy.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
To define an **endothermic reaction**, let's look at what happens during a chemical change from a bond perspective. Every reaction involves two main stages:
1.  **Breaking bonds** in the reactants (this always requires energy).
2.  **Forming bonds** in the products (this always releases energy).

In the provided energy profile for photosynthesis, we can see the products have a higher potential energy than the reactants, indicating that energy has been absorbed overall.

steps.title: Compare bond energy changes
steps.content:
Using the **Bond Energy Perspective**, we compare the energy "cost" of breaking the old bonds to the energy "payback" of making new ones. 

In an endothermic reaction, the energy needed to pull the atoms apart in the reactants is greater than the energy given off when the atoms rearrange into products. This results in a net absorption of energy from the surroundings.

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
The formal definition of an **endothermic reaction** from this perspective is:

A reaction where more energy is required to break the bonds in the reactants than is released when new bonds form in the products.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.2: Energy cycles in reactions**, specifically when calculating enthalpy changes using average bond enthalpies.

parts.content: State and explain whether photosynthesis is an exothermic or endothermic reaction based on the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
• Photosynthesis is endothermic. 1
  • The products are at a higher energy level than the reactants, meaning energy has been absorbed. 1

steps.title: Analyze the energy diagram
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first need to look at the relative positions of the horizontal lines on the energy profile diagram. These lines represent the potential energy of the substances involved in the reaction.

*   The left-hand side represents the **reactants** ($6\mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$).
*   The right-hand side represents the **products** ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6 + 6\mathrm{O}_2$).

This falls under **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**, specifically looking at energy profiles.

steps.title: Compare energy levels
steps.content:
Notice that the products are plotted higher on the vertical axis (Potential Energy) than the reactants. 

$$\text{Energy of Products} > \text{Energy of Reactants}$$

Because the products have more energy than the reactants, the system must have taken in energy from the surroundings during the chemical change. The question equation also gives us a clue: it shows that **light energy** is required for the reaction to proceed.

steps.title: Classify the reaction
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, we define a reaction where the system absorbs energy from its surroundings as **endothermic**. 

Since the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is the difference between the energy of the products and the reactants, a higher product level results in a positive $\Delta H$ value ($+ \Delta H$).

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}} > 0$$

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the visual evidence from the diagram, you can state your final answer to earn the 2 marks:

1.  **State:** Photosynthesis is an **endothermic** reaction.
2.  **Explain:** The **products are at a higher energy level than the reactants**, which indicates that energy has been absorbed from the surroundings (in this case, from light).

steps.title: Analyze the energy diagram
steps.content:
To determine the type of reaction, we first look at the relative potential energy levels of the reactants and products in the diagram. Notice that the energy level of the products ($C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$) is physically higher on the y-axis than the energy level of the reactants ($6CO_2 + 6H_2O$).

This shows that the chemical system gained energy as it moved from the reactant state to the product state.

steps.title: Determine the enthalpy change
steps.content:
The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is defined as the difference between the energy of the products and the energy of the reactants:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Because the products are at a higher energy level, the value of $\Delta H$ is positive ($> 0$). This positive change occurs because the system has absorbed energy from its surroundings—specifically in the form of **light energy**—to reach the higher energy product state.

steps.title: Classify as endothermic
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, any reaction where the system absorbs energy from the surroundings and the products have more enthalpy than the reactants is classified as an **endothermic** reaction.

Therefore, photosynthesis is an **endothermic** reaction because its products are at a higher energy level than its reactants, resulting in a positive $\Delta H$.

parts.content: Explain the role of light energy in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
• Light energy provides the energy required to overcome the high activation energy barrier. 1
  • Without light, the reactants would not have enough energy to reach the transition state. 1

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content:
Take a look at the energy profile diagram provided. You'll notice a large "hill" between the reactants ($6 \mathrm{CO}_2 + 6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$) and the products. This peak represents the **activation energy ($E_a$)**, which is the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur.

In photosynthesis, this energy barrier is quite high, meaning the molecules don't just react on their own under normal conditions.

steps.title: Provide energy for activation
steps.content:
The primary role of light energy is to provide the external energy needed to overcome this high activation energy barrier. 

In chemistry terms, we say that the light energy is absorbed by the system, allowing the reactant molecules to gain enough energy to climb that "hill" on the diagram.

steps.title: Reach the transition state
steps.content:
The very top of the peak on the energy profile is known as the **transition state**. 

Without the input of light energy, the reactant particles ($ \mathrm{CO}_2 $ and $ \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $) would not have enough energy to reach this transition state. If they can't reach the top of the barrier, they cannot transform into the products ($ \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6 $ and $ \mathrm{O}_2 $).

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks for this question, your explanation should include these two key points:

1.  **Light energy provides the energy required** to overcome the high activation energy barrier ($E_a$).
2.  **Without light, the reactants would not have enough energy** to reach the transition state at the peak of the diagram.

This aligns with what we cover in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**, specifically regarding energy profiles and activation energy.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: In the provided energy profile diagram, we can see that the potential energy of the products ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$) is much higher than the potential energy of the reactants ($\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$). This indicates that photosynthesis is a strongly **endothermic** reaction. To learn more about these diagrams, you can review **Topic R1.1: Energy profiles**.

steps.title: Overcome activation energy barrier
steps.content: Because the products are at a higher energy state, the reaction requires a significant input of energy to proceed. Specifically, light energy is absorbed to provide the energy required to overcome the high **activation energy barrier** ($E_a$) shown by the "hump" in the diagram.

steps.title: Reach the transition state
steps.content:
The activation energy represents the minimum energy needed for reactants to reach the **transition state**. Without the absorption of light, the reactant molecules would not have enough energy to reach this peak, and the reaction would not occur. 

In summary:
1. Light energy provides the energy required to overcome the high activation energy barrier.
2. Without light, the reactants would not have enough energy to reach the transition state.

parts.content: State two ways in which the energy profile diagram would change if a catalyst (e.g., an enzyme) were used.
parts.markscheme:
• The activation energy would be lower. 1
  • The peak of the energy curve would be reduced (the transition state occurs at a lower energy). 1

steps.title: Understand the catalyst's role
steps.content:
In kinetics, a **catalyst** (like an enzyme in photosynthesis) works by providing an alternative reaction pathway. This pathway is more efficient than the original one. 

You can review this concept in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**. According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E_a$).

steps.title: Identify activation energy change
steps.content:
The first major change is that the **activation energy** ($E_a$) will be lower. 

On the energy profile diagram, the activation energy is the vertical distance from the energy of the reactants to the top of the peak. By providing an alternative mechanism, the catalyst reduces this "energy barrier."

From the Data Booklet, we can see how this affects the rate constant $k$ in the Arrhenius equation:

$$k = Ae^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

A smaller $E_a$ results in a larger rate constant $k$, meaning the reaction proceeds faster.

steps.title: Identify transition state change
steps.content:
The second change relates to the **peak of the curve**. Because the activation energy is lower, the maximum energy level reached during the reaction—the energy of the **transition state**—is reduced.

On the graph, you would draw a new dashed line that curves below the original solid line, starting and ending at the same reactant and product levels, but with a much lower "hump."

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you must state both distinct visual changes to the diagram:

1.  **Lower activation energy**: The value of $E_a$ decreases.
2.  **Lower peak/transition state**: The maximum point on the curve is lower in energy.

Note: The enthalpy change ($ΔH$) between reactants and products remains **unchanged**.

parts.content: Suggest why photosynthesis would not occur in the dark, even if all reactants are present.
parts.markscheme: Photosynthesis needs light energy to overcome the activation energy; in the dark, there is no energy input to start the reaction. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content:
Looking at the provided energy profile diagram, notice the large peak between the reactants (left side) and the products (right side). This peak represents the **activation energy** ($E_a$).

In **Topic R2.2: How fast?**, we learn that even if a reaction is overall possible, it cannot proceed unless the reactants gain enough energy to reach this high-energy "transition state" at the top of the curve.

steps.title: Define activation energy requirements
steps.content:
The activation energy is the minimum energy that colliding molecules must possess for a reaction to occur. We can see this concept reflected in the Arrhenius equation found in the data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{RT}}$$

This equation shows that the rate of reaction ($k$) depends heavily on the activation energy ($E_a$). If there isn't enough energy available to overcome this barrier, the reaction rate will be effectively zero.

steps.title: Relate light to energy input
steps.content:
In the chemical equation for photosynthesis, light energy is specified as a requirement. Light provides the necessary energy to "lift" the reactants over the activation energy barrier shown in the diagram.

$$\text{light energy} \rightarrow \text{Activation Energy (} E_a \text{)}$$

steps.title: Explain the dark conditions
steps.content:
If the plant is in the dark, there is no light energy input. Without this external energy source, the reactant molecules ($\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) do not have enough energy to reach the transition state at the top of the energy profile. 

Consequently, they cannot overcome the **activation energy** barrier, so the reaction does not occur.

highlights.text: Suggest why photosynthesis would not occur in the dark, even if all reactants are present.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: Looking at the energy profile diagram, we can see that the potential energy of the products is higher than that of the reactants. This indicates that the reaction is **endothermic**, meaning it results in a net absorption of energy.

steps.title: Identify the energy requirement
steps.content:
In an endothermic reaction, the system must take in energy from the surroundings to move from a lower energy state to a higher energy state. This is often referred to as an "uphill" process. This energy gap is related to the enthalpy change ($ΔH$):

$$\Delta H^{\ominus}=\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products})-\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

Because the products have a higher enthalpy, $\Delta H$ is positive, and the reaction requires a continuous external energy input to proceed.

steps.title: Explain the role of light
steps.content:
The chemical equation shows that **light energy** is the specific source used to drive this reaction upward. Light provides the necessary energy to overcome the activation energy ($E_a$) and reach the higher energy state of the products. 

In the dark, there is no external energy source to provide this input. Without this "push," the reactants cannot overcome the energy barrier or the endothermic requirement, so the reaction will not occur.

highlights.text: would not occur in the dark

parts.content: State the difference between heat and temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- State that heat is the energy transferred from one body to another due to a temperature difference, whereas temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define Temperature
steps.content:
In chemistry and physics, **temperature** is a property related to the motion of particles within a substance. According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (Topic S1.1.2), temperature is defined as a measure of the average kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the particles in a substance. 

As the student heats the water, the particles move faster, which corresponds to an increase in temperature.

steps.title: Define Heat
steps.content: **Heat** (or thermal energy transfer) is not a property stored within a substance, but rather the **energy in transit**. It is the flow of energy from a body at a higher temperature (like the Bunsen burner flame) to a body at a lower temperature (the distilled water). This transfer occurs because of the temperature gradient between the two systems.

steps.title: State the final difference
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must clearly distinguish between the "measure" (temperature) and the "transfer" (heat).

**Heat** is the energy transferred between substances due to a temperature difference, whereas **temperature** is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance.

highlights.text: State the difference between heat and temperature.

parts.content: Identify whether heating water is a physical or chemical change.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the process as a physical change <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze chemical identity
steps.content: To determine if a change is physical or chemical, we look at the chemical identity of the substance. When we are **heating water**, the liquid consists of $H_{2}O$ molecules. As the temperature increases, these molecules simply move faster (increasing their kinetic energy), but they remain $H_{2}O$.

steps.title: Check for new substances
steps.content: A chemical change requires the breaking of intramolecular bonds (the bonds *inside* the molecule) to form entirely new substances. In this process, no new chemical species are created; we do not see the formation of hydrogen gas ($H_{2}$) or oxygen gas ($O_{2}$). Since the chemical composition is unchanged, this is a characteristic of a physical change.

steps.title: State the final classification
steps.content: Because the substance starts as water and ends as water (just at a higher temperature), we classify this process as a **physical change**.

highlights.text: Identify the process as a physical change

steps.title: Analyze the heating process
steps.content: When the student is **heating** the distilled water, energy is being transferred to the water molecules. This increases their average kinetic energy, which we observe as a rise in temperature. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Test for reversibility
steps.content:
To determine the type of change using **Reversibility Reasoning**, we ask: *Can this process be easily undone?*

If we remove the Bunsen burner and allow the beaker to cool, the water returns to its original state and temperature. Because we can restore the water to its initial condition simply by reversing the temperature change, the process is highly reversible.

steps.title: Check chemical identity
steps.content: In a physical change, the chemical composition of the substance remains the same. Throughout the heating and cooling process, the substance remains $H_{2}O$. No chemical bonds within the water molecules are broken, and no new chemical substances are formed.

steps.title: Identify the change type
steps.content:
Since the process is easily reversible and the chemical identity of the water does not change, we categorize this as a **physical change**.

According to the markscheme, identifying this as a physical change earns the mark.

highlights.text: Identify the process as a physical change

parts.content: Explain why the temperature of the water increases as it is heated.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that heat energy is transferred from the flame to the water <marks>M1</marks>
- Explain that the water particles gain kinetic energy, leading to increased motion or vibration which raises the temperature: $\text{gain in kinetic energy} \Rightarrow \text{increased motion/vibration} \Rightarrow \text{rise in temperature}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Energy transfer from source
steps.content: First, we need to recognize the direction of energy flow. When you **place a beaker of distilled water over a Bunsen burner**, thermal energy (heat) is transferred from the high-temperature flame to the cooler water inside the beaker.

highlights.text: place a beaker of distilled water over a Bunsen burner

steps.title: Increase in kinetic energy
steps.content: According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (Topic S1.1.2), as the water molecules absorb this thermal energy, they begin to move more rapidly. This means the molecules gain **kinetic energy**, resulting in increased translational motion and vibration within the liquid.

steps.title: Relating motion to temperature
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry (Topic S1.1.3), temperature is defined as a measure of the **average kinetic energy** of the particles in a substance. 

$$\text{Energy transfer} \Rightarrow \uparrow \text{Average Kinetic Energy} \Rightarrow \uparrow \text{Temperature}$$

Because the water molecules are moving faster on average, the macroscopic measurement we call temperature increases.

parts.content: State the physical state and movement of water particles before and after heating.
parts.markscheme:
- State that before heating, water is in a liquid state with particles close together and moving slowly or randomly <marks>A1</marks>
- State that after heating, the water remains in a liquid state but the particles move significantly faster with more energy <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the initial state
steps.content: Before heating, the **distilled water** is in a **liquid state**. According to the Kinetic Molecular Theory (Topic S1.1.2), particles in a liquid are close together but have enough energy to move past one another randomly and relatively slowly.

steps.title: Analyze the heating process
steps.content: As the Bunsen burner is used to **heat it**, thermal energy is transferred to the water. In Chemistry, we know that temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the particles. Therefore, increasing the temperature increases the energy of the particles.

steps.title: Describe particles after heating
steps.content: After heating, the water **remains in a liquid state** (as it is being prepared for a solution and hasn't reached boiling point). However, because the average kinetic energy has increased, the particles now move **significantly faster** than they did before.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure you address both the state and the movement for both conditions:

1.  **Before heating:** Liquid state; particles are close together and moving slowly/randomly.
2.  **After heating:** Liquid state; particles move significantly faster with more kinetic energy.

parts.content: The student accidentally leaves the beaker boiling. Suggest one safety precaution to prevent accidents from overheating.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggest a safety precaution such as using a heatproof mat, wearing safety goggles, or never leaving the apparatus unattended <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the risk
steps.content:
When a beaker of liquid is left to boil uncontrollably, two major risks arise: 
1. **Splashing (Bumping):** The solution can suddenly bubble over or splash out of the beaker.
2. **Shattering:** If the water evaporates completely, the dry glass can overheat and shatter due to thermal stress.

To protect yourself from these physical hazards, you need to use personal protective equipment (PPE).

steps.title: Select safety goggles
steps.content:
The most important piece of PPE in this scenario is **safety goggles**. 

In the event of a splash or the beaker shattering, goggles provide a physical barrier that protects your eyes from hot salt solution or flying glass fragments. This is a fundamental safety requirement in any IB Chemistry lab to prevent permanent eye injury.

steps.title: Final recommendation
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, any of the following would earn the mark, but wearing safety goggles is the primary PPE recommendation:

- **Wear safety goggles** (to protect eyes from splashes or shattering glass).
- Use a heatproof mat (to protect the bench).
- Never leave the apparatus unattended (to monitor the boiling level).

For this specific PPE-focused approach, the best answer is to ensure you are wearing safety goggles.

steps.title: Identify the thermal hazard
steps.content: When a student is heating a solution and accidentally leaves it boiling, the temperature of the apparatus and the surrounding area increases significantly. If the liquid evaporates completely, the glassware may crack, and the intense heat from the Bunsen burner can damage the laboratory bench surface.

steps.title: Modify the bench setup
steps.content: Using the **Equipment and Setup** approach, we look for physical modifications to the workstation. A primary precaution is to place a **heatproof mat** underneath the Bunsen burner and tripod. This mat acts as a thermal insulator, protecting the bench from scorching or catching fire due to the prolonged heat exposure.

steps.title: State the final precaution
steps.content:
To prevent accidents from overheating and to protect the laboratory infrastructure, the student should use a **heatproof mat**.

Other setup-related precautions include ensuring the beaker is placed securely on a wire gauze atop a tripod to distribute heat evenly, which helps prevent the glass from shattering (thermal shock).

steps.title: Analyze the risk
steps.content: The question describes a scenario where a beaker of water is being heated over a Bunsen burner. The primary risk identified is that the student "accidentally leaves the beaker boiling," which can lead to the solution boiling dry, the glass cracking, or the contents splashing (bumping).

steps.title: Apply procedural safety
steps.content: Using a **procedural conduct** approach, we focus on the behavior of the experimenter. The most effective way to prevent accidents during heating is to ensure constant supervision of the apparatus. This allows the student to intervene immediately if the boiling becomes too vigorous.

steps.title: Formulate the precaution
steps.content:
To prevent overheating and subsequent accidents, the student should **never leave the apparatus unattended while it is being heated**. 

This ensures that the heat source (the Bunsen burner) can be turned off or the flame adjusted to a "safety flame" immediately if the water level gets too low or if the boiling becomes unstable.

steps.title: Verify with markscheme
steps.content: According to the markscheme, behavioral precautions are acceptable for this mark. By suggesting that the student remains present, you address the root cause of the accident described in the prompt.

parts.content: State the specific heat capacity of water.
parts.markscheme:
$4.18\ \mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ {}^{\circ}C^{-1}}$
<marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

methods.label: Direct Recall or Data Booklet Reference

steps.title: Identify the required constant
steps.content:
The question asks for the **specific heat capacity of water**. This is a fundamental constant used in thermochemistry to calculate the energy required to change the temperature of water. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**, where we use the formula $q = mc\Delta T$. To solve problems in this topic, you must know the value of $c$ (specific heat capacity).

highlights.text: State the specific heat capacity of water.

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
While many students memorize this value through practice, it is officially provided in the **IB Chemistry Data Booklet**. 

You can find this value in **Section 1: Physical constants and unit conversions**. In the IB curriculum, the value for the specific heat capacity of water is standardized to three significant figures.

steps.title: State the final value
steps.content:
According to the data booklet and standard IB marking practice, the specific heat capacity of water is:

$$c = 4.18\ \mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ {}^{\circ}C^{-1}}$$

Note that the units can also be written as $\mathrm{J\ g^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$ because a change of $1\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\ \mathrm{K}$.

parts.content: Calculate the heat absorbed by the water using the expression $Q = mc\Delta T$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the values into the formula: $Q = 100 \times 4.18 \times 10$
<marks>M1</marks>
- State the final value with the correct unit: $4180\ \mathrm{J}$
<marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the known variables
steps.content:
To calculate the heat absorbed, we first need to extract the values for mass ($m$) and temperature change ($

\Delta T$) from the question. 

- Mass of water ($m$) = $100\ \text{g}$
- Temperature change ($\Delta T$) = $10\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Specific heat capacity of water ($c$) = $4.18\ \text{J/g}^{\circ}\text{C}$

This topic is covered in **Reactivity 1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**, which explains how energy is transferred during temperature changes.

steps.title: Substitute into the formula
steps.content:
Now we use the expression provided in the question: 

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

By substituting our values directly into this equation, we set up the calculation to find the energy ($Q$) in Joules:

$$Q = 100 \times 4.18 \times 10$$

steps.title: Calculate final heat energy
steps.content:
Finally, perform the multiplication to find the total heat absorbed. 

$$Q = 4180\ \text{J}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct substitution and one mark for the correct final value with units.

gdc.instructions: Type the product into the calculation area.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into the keypad.

calculator.description: Calculate the total heat energy by multiplying mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

parts.content: Suggest two reasons why the calculated value of $Q$ might be lower than the actual energy released by the burner.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggest that heat is lost to the surroundings or not all heat is transferred to the water
<marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest that there is incomplete combustion or heat is absorbed by the beaker/apparatus
<marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the calculation method
steps.content:
To understand why the calculated energy ($Q$) is low, we first look at how it is calculated in the lab. Usually, students use the specific heat capacity formula:

$$Q = mcΔT$$

In this experiment, $m$ is the mass of the water ($100\ \mathrm{g}$), $c$ is the specific heat capacity of water, and $\Delta T$ is the $10\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$ rise. This calculation assumes that **100%** of the thermal energy released by the spirit burner is absorbed solely by the water. If any energy follows a different physical pathway, our calculated $Q$ will be lower than the actual energy released.

steps.title: Heat loss to surroundings
steps.content:
The first physical pathway for energy loss is dissipation into the environment. As the spirit burner burns, not all the flame's heat is directed into the bottom of the beaker. Some energy is lost to the surrounding air via **convection** (hot air rising away from the beaker) and **radiation**.

Because this energy never enters the water, it does not contribute to the temperature rise, leading to an underestimate of the total energy released.

highlights.text: heat is lost to the surroundings or not all heat is transferred to the water

steps.title: Absorption by the apparatus
steps.content:
Another pathway is the absorption of heat by the equipment itself. For the water to heat up, the **beaker** must also be heated. Additionally, the **thermometer** used to measure the change absorbs a small amount of thermal energy.

Since the formula $Q = mcΔT$ only accounts for the mass of the water, the energy used to heat the glass beaker is "missing" from our final result.

highlights.text: heat is absorbed by the beaker/apparatus

steps.title: State the final reasons
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of heat transfer pathways, the two main reasons are:
1. **Heat is lost to the surroundings** (the air around the beaker).
2. **Heat is absorbed by the apparatus** (the beaker or the thermometer).

steps.title: Analyze the energy gap
steps.content: In a calorimetry experiment, we use the formula $Q = mc\Delta T$ to calculate the heat absorbed by the water. However, this value is almost always lower than the theoretical energy released by the fuel. To understand why, we need to look at both the chemical process in the burner and the physical setup of the experiment.

highlights.text: calculated value of Q might be lower than the actual energy released

steps.title: Reason 1: Incomplete combustion
steps.content:
A major chemical factor is **incomplete combustion**. In a school lab, the spirit burner often doesn't have a perfectly efficient oxygen supply. 

Instead of the fuel reacting completely to produce $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, it may produce carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) or soot (carbon, $\text{C}$). These partial reactions release significantly less energy per mole of fuel than complete combustion, meaning the burner produces less heat than expected from its theoretical enthalpy of combustion.

You can review this concept in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Reason 2: Heat loss
steps.content:
Even the heat that *is* produced isn't all captured by the water. There are two main physical factors here:

1.  **Heat loss to the surroundings:** Much of the flame's energy warms the surrounding air or is carried away by convection currents rather than entering the beaker.
2.  **Heat absorbed by the apparatus:** The glass beaker itself has a heat capacity. Some of the energy is used to raise the temperature of the beaker, but our calculation ($Q = mc\Delta T$) only accounts for the mass of the water.

These considerations are part of the practical limitations discussed in **Topic R1.1.5: Practical considerations**.

highlights.text: heat is lost to the surroundings
heat is absorbed by the beaker/apparatus

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you should suggest one chemical reason and one physical reason:

*   **Reason 1:** Heat loss to the surroundings (or energy used to heat the beaker).
*   **Reason 2:** Incomplete combustion of the fuel.

parts.content: Identify one way the student could improve the accuracy of this measurement.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the use of an insulating lid or surrounding the beaker with insulating material
<marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the source of error
steps.content: In a calorimetry experiment like this, the biggest source of error is heat loss to the surroundings. When heating water in a beaker, energy from the spirit burner escapes into the air rather than being transferred entirely to the water. This leads to a lower temperature rise than expected, which decreases the accuracy of the final results.

steps.title: Apply physical insulation
steps.content:
To improve accuracy, we need to ensure as much heat as possible stays inside the system. By using **physical insulation**, we create a barrier that minimizes heat transfer to the environment. 

This can be done in two main ways:
1. Adding an **insulating lid** to the beaker to prevent heat loss from the water's surface (evaporative and convective cooling).
2. Wrapping the beaker in an **insulating material**, such as mineral wool or foam, to reduce heat loss through the glass walls.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Following the **Physical Insulation** method, the most effective improvement is to use an insulating lid or surround the beaker with insulating material.

According to the markscheme, either of these suggestions would earn the mark.

highlights.text: Identify the use of an insulating lid or surrounding the beaker with insulating material

steps.title: Identify the source of error
steps.content: In calorimetry experiments using a spirit burner, a major source of inaccuracy is **heat loss to the surroundings**. Not all the energy released by the burning fuel is absorbed by the $100\ \mathrm{g}$ of water; some is lost to the air, especially if there are drafts in the room.

steps.title: Apply draft shields
steps.content:
To improve the accuracy, the student should use **draft shields**. These are physical barriers placed around the spirit burner and the beaker. 

By surrounding the apparatus, you create a controlled environment that minimizes the impact of air currents that would otherwise 'blow' the heat away from the beaker.

steps.title: Explain the improvement
steps.content:
Using draft shields ensures that a higher proportion of the heat generated by the combustion reaction is directed specifically toward the bottom and sides of the beaker. This reduces the systematic error associated with heat loss, making the calculated enthalpy change more accurate.

This falls under **Topic R1.1.5: Practical considerations** in the IB syllabus, which emphasizes minimizing heat loss in experimental thermochemistry.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: One way to improve the accuracy is to **place draft shields around the spirit burner and the beaker** to prevent air currents from redirecting heat away from the water.

steps.title: Analyze the current measurement
steps.content:
In this experiment, the student is measuring the temperature change of water to calculate energy transfer. Currently, the measurement of the "temperature of the water rises by $10\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$" likely relies on a manual thermometer. Manual thermometers are limited by human eyesight (parallax error) and have a fixed precision (usually $\pm 0.5\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$ or $\pm 0.1\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$).

To improve the accuracy and precision of the data, we need a method that reduces these human errors and provides more detailed readings.

highlights.text: temperature of the water rises by 10\ ^{\circ}C

steps.title: Introduce high-resolution tools
steps.content:
A significant improvement is to replace the manual thermometer with a **digital temperature probe** connected to a **data logger**.

This approach aligns with **Topic R1.1.5: Practical considerations** in your syllabus, which focuses on identifying errors and suggesting improvements in calorimetry experiments.

steps.title: Improve precision and resolution
steps.content:
Digital probes offer higher **resolution** than standard lab thermometers. While a bulb thermometer might only allow you to read to the nearest $0.5\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$, a digital probe can often provide readings to $0.01\ \mathrm{^{\circ}C}$. 

This reduces the percentage uncertainty in your temperature change ($\Delta T$) calculation.

steps.title: Capture the peak temperature
steps.content:
Using a data logger allows for continuous monitoring. In calorimetry, heat is constantly lost to the surroundings. A data logger records multiple data points per second, ensuring you capture the **true maximum temperature** before the water begins to cool down. 

This is much more accurate than a student trying to spot the highest point on a moving liquid column by eye.

steps.title: Final recommendation
steps.content:
Therefore, to improve the measurement, the student should use a **digital temperature probe** or **data logger**. 

While the markscheme specifically mentions insulation to prevent heat loss, using higher-resolution instrumentation is another scientifically valid way to improve the accuracy and reliability of the recorded temperature rise.

questions.specification: When $100.0\ \text{cm}^3$ of $1.00\ \text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3}$ HCl is mixed with $100.0\ \text{cm}^3$ of $1.00\ \text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3}$ NaOH, the temperature rises by $7.2\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Assuming total mass = $200\ \text{g}$ and $c = 4.18\ \text{J}\ \text{g}^{-1}\ \text{K}^{-1}$, what is $\Delta H_{\text{neut}}$ in $\text{kJ}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$?
