parts.content: Suggest two reasons why the student carried out repeat trials.
parts.markscheme:
Any two of:
to reduce the effect of random errors <marks>A1</marks>
to identify/eliminate outliers/anomalies <marks>A1</marks>
to calculate an average value <marks>A1</marks>
to increase the reliability of the results <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>2</marks>

steps.title: Understand the experimental context
steps.content: In IB Chemistry, especially in Topic **R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change**, experiments like calorimetry are prone to various types of uncertainty. The student in this question performed the experiment four times rather than just once. This is a standard procedure designed to improve the quality of the final result.

steps.title: Manage random errors
steps.content: One primary reason for repeats is to **reduce the effect of random errors**. Random errors are unpredictable fluctuations in measurements, such as slight variations in reading a thermometer or measuring a volume of $\text{HCl}$. While we cannot eliminate them, performing multiple trials and taking an average ensures that these fluctuations tend to cancel each other out.

steps.title: Identify and eliminate anomalies
steps.content: Repeating trials allows a student to **identify and eliminate outliers (anomalies)**. If one trial produces a $\Delta H$ value significantly different from the others (perhaps due to heat loss or a spill), having multiple trials makes it obvious which result is incorrect. This trial can then be excluded from final calculations.

steps.title: Improve reliability and averages
steps.content: By collecting more data, the student can **calculate an average value**, which provides a better estimate of the true enthalpy change than any single trial. This process directly leads to an **increase in the reliability** of the results, as the final conclusion is supported by a consistent set of data points.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you can suggest any two of these scientific justifications:

1.  **To reduce the effect of random errors.**
2.  **To identify or eliminate outliers/anomalies.**
3.  **To calculate an average value.**
4.  **To increase the reliability of the results.**

parts.content: Outline why the student uses a polystyrene cup for the reaction.
parts.markscheme:
It is a good (thermal) insulator / has a low thermal conductivity OR to reduce/prevent heat loss to the surroundings. <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the experimental goal
steps.content:
To determine the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) of the reaction between $\text{MgO}$ and $\text{HCl}$, we need to measure the temperature change ($\Delta T$) of the mixture. This experiment relies on the assumption that all the heat energy produced by the reaction stays within the reaction mixture to be measured by the thermometer.

This falls under **Reactivity 1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in your syllabus, where understanding practical considerations is key to accurate results.

steps.title: Define thermal insulation
steps.content: Polystyrene is used because it is a **good thermal insulator**. Materials that are good insulators have **low thermal conductivity**, meaning they do not allow heat energy to pass through them easily.

steps.title: Minimize heat exchange
steps.content:
In a calorimetry experiment, the main source of error is often heat lost to the surroundings. By using a polystyrene cup, the student **minimizes heat exchange** between the reaction mixture and the environment. 

This ensures that the temperature data collected is as accurate as possible, which is essential for calculating the correct enthalpy change using the heat energy formula:

$$q = mc\Delta T$$

steps.title: State the final reason
steps.content: Therefore, the student uses a polystyrene cup because it reduces heat loss to the surroundings, ensuring that the measured temperature rise reflects the true energy change of the chemical reaction.

parts.content:
Data for the reaction is shown in the table below.

| Trial | Volume of $1.0\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ $\text{HCl}$ ($\text{cm}^3$) | Mass of $\text{MgO}$ (g) | Initial Temp ($^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Final Temp ($^{\circ}\text{C}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 100.0 | 0.25 | 25.5 | 26.5 |
| 2 | 100.0 | 0.50 | 25.0 | 29.1 |
| 3 | 100.0 | 0.25 | 26.0 | 28.1 |
| 4 | 100.0 | 0.50 | 24.1 | 28.1 |

Determine which is the limiting reactant in all four trials.
parts.markscheme:
$n(\text{HCl}) = 1.0\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.1000\text{ dm}^3 = 0.10\text{ mol}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Trial 1 & 3: $n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.25\text{ g}}{40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0062\text{ mol}$
Trial 2 & 4: $n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.50\text{ g}}{40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.012\text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

$0.10\text{ mol}$ of $\text{HCl}$ reacts with $0.050\text{ mol}$ of $\text{MgO}$, so $\text{MgO}$ is the limiting reactant in all trials. <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>3</marks>

steps.title: Identify the stoichiometric ratio
steps.content:
To find the limiting reactant, we first look at the balanced chemical equation to determine the molar ratio between the reactants.

$$\text{MgO(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

The coefficients tell us that **1 mole of MgO** reacts with **2 moles of HCl**. This $1:2$ ratio is crucial for our comparison.

steps.title: Calculate moles of HCl
steps.content:
In all four trials, the volume and concentration of $\text{HCl}$ remain constant. We use the formula for molar concentration from the data booklet:

$$n = CV$$

First, convert the volume from $\text{cm}^3$ to $\text{dm}^3$ by dividing by $1000$:
$$V = \frac{100.0 \text{ cm}^3}{1000} = 0.1000 \text{ dm}^3$$

Now, calculate the moles ($n$):
$$n(\text{HCl}) = 1.0 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.1000 \text{ dm}^3 = 0.10 \text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the concentration multiplied by the volume divided by 1000.

calculator.description: Calculate moles of HCl by multiplying concentration by volume in dm³.

steps.title: Calculate moles of MgO
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the moles of $\text{MgO}$ for the two different masses used in the trials ($0.25\text{ g}$ and $0.50\text{ g}$) using the formula:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

The molar mass ($M$) of $\text{MgO}$ is $24.31 (\text{Mg}) + 16.00 (\text{O}) = 40.31 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

**For Trials 1 and 3 (0.25 g):**
$$n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.25 \text{ g}}{40.31 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0062 \text{ mol}$$

**For Trials 2 and 4 (0.50 g):**
$$n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.50 \text{ g}}{40.31 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.012 \text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass by the molar mass (40.31).

calculator.description: Calculate moles for both mass values of MgO.

steps.title: Compare moles and conclude
steps.content:
According to the $1:2$ ratio, $0.10\text{ mol}$ of $\text{HCl}$ would require half that amount of $\text{MgO}$ to react completely:

$$\text{Required } n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.10 \text{ mol HCl}}{2} = 0.050 \text{ mol MgO}$$

Now we compare the **available** moles of $\text{MgO}$ to the **required** amount:
- In all trials, the available $\text{MgO}$ ($0.0062\text{ mol}$ or $0.012\text{ mol}$) is significantly less than the $0.050\text{ mol}$ required.

Since there is not enough $\text{MgO}$ to react with all the $\text{HCl}$, **MgO is the limiting reactant** in all four trials.

steps.title: Calculate moles of HCl available
steps.content:
First, we find the number of moles of $\text{HCl}$ used in all trials. All four trials use the same volume and concentration of acid.

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$\begin{aligned} n &= CV \end{aligned}$$

Convert volume to $\text{dm}^3$:
$$100.0\text{ cm}^3 = 0.1000\text{ dm}^3$$

Calculate moles:
$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{HCl}) &= 1.0\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.1000\text{ dm}^3 \\ n(\text{HCl}) &= 0.10\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the concentration by the volume in liters.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the concentration by the volume in liters.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the concentration by the volume in liters.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of HCl

steps.title: Find the stoichiometric mass needed
steps.content:
According to the balanced equation, $1\text{ mole}$ of $\text{MgO}$ reacts with $2\text{ moles}$ of $\text{HCl}$.

$$\text{MgO(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

1. **Calculate moles of $\text{MgO}$ needed**:
   To react with $0.10\text{ mol}$ of $\text{HCl}$, we need half as many moles of $\text{MgO}$:
   $$n(\text{MgO})_{\text{required}} = \frac{0.10\text{ mol}}{2} = 0.050\text{ mol}$$

2. **Calculate the "Theoretical Mass" of $\text{MgO}$**:
   The molar mass of $\text{MgO}$ is $24.31 + 16.00 = 40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}$.
   $$\begin{aligned} m(\text{MgO})_{\text{theoretical}} &= n \times M \\ &= 0.050\text{ mol} \times 40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ &= 2.0155\text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate moles required (0.05) and multiply by molar mass (40.31).

ti84.instructions: Calculate moles required (0.05) and multiply by molar mass (40.31).

desmos.instructions: Calculate moles required (0.05) and multiply by molar mass (40.31).

calculator.description: Calculate the required mass of MgO to react with all HCl

steps.title: Compare theoretical to experimental mass
steps.content:
Now we compare this theoretical mass ($2.0155\text{ g}$) to the actual mass of $\text{MgO}$ used in the trials to see if we have enough.

*   **Trials 1 & 3**: The mass used is $0.25\text{ g}$.
*   **Trials 2 & 4**: The mass used is $0.50\text{ g}$.

In all trials, the actual mass used ($0.25\text{ g}$ or $0.50\text{ g}$) is significantly **less** than the $2.0155\text{ g}$ required to consume all the $\text{HCl}$.

steps.title: Identify the limiting reactant
steps.content:
Since the mass of $\text{MgO}$ provided in every trial is less than the mass required to react with the $0.10\text{ mol}$ of $\text{HCl}$, the $\text{MgO}$ will be completely consumed before the $\text{HCl}$ runs out.

Therefore, **MgO is the limiting reactant** in all four trials.

This confirms the markscheme requirement that the moles of $\text{MgO}$ used ($0.0062$ or $0.012\text{ mol}$) are much smaller than the stoichiometric requirement of $0.050\text{ mol}$.

steps.title: Analyze the stoichiometry
steps.content:
To find the limiting reactant, we first look at the balanced chemical equation provided in the question:

$$\text{MgO(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

This tells us that 1 mole of $\text{MgO}$ reacts with exactly 2 moles of $\text{HCl}$. To show that $\text{HCl}$ is in excess, we will calculate how much volume is actually needed to react with the *maximum* amount of $\text{MgO}$ used in the trials ($0.50\text{ g}$ in Trials 2 and 4). 

This concept is part of **Topic R2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Calculate moles of MgO
steps.content:
First, let's find the number of moles in the maximum mass of $\text{MgO}$ used ($0.50\text{ g}$). We use the molar mass of $\text{MgO}$ ($24.31 + 16.00 = 40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) and the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.50\text{ g}}{40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0124\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Determine required HCl moles
steps.content:
Based on the $1:2$ molar ratio from the balanced equation, we can calculate the moles of $\text{HCl}$ required to react with all of the $0.50\text{ g}$ of $\text{MgO}$:

$$\text{Moles of HCl needed} = 2 \times n(\text{MgO})$$
$$\text{Moles of HCl needed} = 2 \times 0.0124\text{ mol} = 0.0248\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate required HCl volume
steps.content:
Now, we find the volume of $1.0\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ $\text{HCl}$ that contains this many moles. Using the concentration formula from the data booklet:

$$n = CV \Rightarrow V = \frac{n}{C}$$

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{0.0248\text{ mol}}{1.0\text{ mol dm}^{-3}} \\ &= 0.0248\text{ dm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

Converting to $\text{cm}^3$:
$$V = 0.0248\text{ dm}^3 \times 1000 = 24.8\text{ cm}^3$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the following calculation on the home screen:

calculator.description: Calculate the volume of HCl in cm³ needed for 0.50g of MgO.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
We compare the required volume to the volume used in the experiment:
- **Required Volume** (for $0.50\text{ g}$ $\text{MgO}$): $24.8\text{ cm}^3$
- **Actual Volume Used**: $100.0\text{ cm}^3$

Since $100.0\text{ cm}^3$ is much greater than $24.8\text{ cm}^3$, the $\text{HCl}$ is in excess in all trials (even the ones with $0.50\text{ g}$ $\text{MgO}$). For the $0.25\text{ g}$ trials, even less volume ($12.4\text{ cm}^3$) is needed.

Therefore, **MgO is the limiting reactant** in all four trials.

parts.content: Determine which trial has data that is inconsistent with the data from the other three trials, giving a reason for your answer.
parts.markscheme:
Trial 1 <marks>A1</marks>

Its temperature change ($\Delta T = 1.0\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$) is significantly lower than Trial 3 ($\Delta T = 2.1\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$) which used the same mass of $\text{MgO}$ / the temperature rise is not proportional to the mass of $\text{MgO}$ compared to other trials. <marks>R1</marks>

<marks>2</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the experimental method
steps.content:
To identify an inconsistent trial using the **Direct Comparison of Identical Masses** approach, we need to find two trials where the student used the exact same mass of $\text{MgO}$ and compare their resulting temperature changes ($\Delta T$). 

This falls under **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in your IB syllabus. In a calorimetry experiment like this, if the mass of the reactant is the same, we expect a similar temperature change.

steps.title: Identify identical trials
steps.content:
By looking at the data provided for the experiment (as noted in the markscheme), we can see that **Trial 1** and **Trial 3** both used the same mass of $\text{MgO}$. 

Because the independent variable (mass of $\text{MgO}$) is identical for these two trials, the dependent variable (temperature change) should also be very similar if the data is consistent.

steps.title: Compare temperature changes
steps.content:
Now, let's compare the recorded temperature changes for these two trials:

*   **Trial 1**: $\Delta T = 1.0\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$
*   **Trial 3**: $\Delta T = 2.1\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$

Despite using the same amount of magnesium oxide, the temperature rise in Trial 3 is more than double the rise recorded in Trial 1.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the temperature change of Trial 1 from Trial 3.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the temperature change of Trial 1 from Trial 3.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference in temperature change between the two trials.

steps.title: Identify the inconsistent trial
steps.content:
Since Trial 1 shows a temperature change that is significantly lower than Trial 3 for the same mass of reactant, **Trial 1** is the inconsistent data point.

**Reason:** Its temperature change is significantly lower than that of Trial 3, which used the same mass of $\text{MgO}$.

steps.title: Understand the proportional relationship
steps.content:
In a calorimetry experiment, the temperature change ($\Delta T$) is directly proportional to the amount of heat released ($q$). Since the heat released depends on the amount of reactant, the ratio of temperature change to the mass of $\text{MgO}$ should remain constant across all trials if the data is consistent:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\Delta T}{\text{mass of MgO}}$$

This relationship is derived from the calorimetry equation:

$$q = mc\Delta T$$

Where $q$ is proportional to the moles (and thus mass) of the limiting reactant.

steps.title: Compare Trial 1 and Trial 3
steps.content:
We are given that Trial 1 and Trial 3 used the same mass of $\text{MgO}$. Let's look at their temperature changes:

- **Trial 1**: $\Delta T = 1.0\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Trial 3**: $\Delta T = 2.1\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$

If we assume a mass ($m$), the ratio for Trial 1 is $\frac{1.0}{m}$ and for Trial 3 it is $\frac{2.1}{m}$. The ratio for Trial 3 is more than double that of Trial 1, despite using the same mass of reactant.

gdc.instructions: Perform 2.1 / 1.0 to find the scale factor between the trials.

ti84.instructions: Divide 2.1 by 1.0 to see the ratio of temperature change for the same mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the factor difference between Trial 3 and Trial 1 temperature changes.

steps.title: Identify the inconsistent trial
steps.content:
Since Trial 3 aligns with the expected trend of the other trials (where temperature rise is proportional to mass), **Trial 1** is the inconsistent data point. 

The reason is that its temperature change is significantly lower than that of Trial 3, even though they used the same mass of $\text{MgO}$. This suggests a possible experimental error in Trial 1, such as poor insulation or incomplete reaction.

parts.content: Calculate the magnitude of the heat change, $Q$, and the $\Delta H$ for the reaction in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ using the data from trial 4. Assume the specific heat capacity of the solution is $4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$ and the density is $1.00\text{ g cm}^{-3}$.
parts.markscheme:
$Q = mc\Delta T = (100.0\text{ g} + 0.50\text{ g}) \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1} \times (28.1 - 24.1)\text{ K} = 1680\text{ J}$ (or $1.68\text{ kJ}$) <marks>M1</marks>

$n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.50\text{ g}}{40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0124\text{ mol}$
$\Delta H = -\frac{1.68\text{ kJ}}{0.0124\text{ mol}} = -135\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

*Award [2] for correct final answer. Accept range -135 to -140 depending on rounding of moles.*

<marks>2</marks>

steps.title: Identify mass and temperature change
steps.content:
To calculate the heat change, we first need the total mass of the system and the change in temperature. In the **Combined Mass Approach**, we add the mass of the solid MgO to the mass of the HCl solution.

$$\begin{aligned} m_{total} &= m(\text{HCl}) + m(\text{MgO}) \\ m_{total} &= 100.00\text{ g} + 0.50\text{ g} = 100.50\text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

Next, calculate the temperature change ($\Delta T$) for Trial 4:

$$\Delta T = 28.1\text{ }^\circ\text{C} - 24.1\text{ }^\circ\text{C} = 4.0\text{ K}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature.

ti84.instructions: Enter the final temperature minus the initial temperature.

calculator.description: Calculate the temperature change.

steps.title: Calculate heat change magnitude
steps.content:
Now we calculate the magnitude of the heat change ($Q$) using the formula for heat transfer. 

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= 100.50\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1} \times 4.0\text{ K} \\ Q &= 1680.36\text{ J} \approx 1.68\text{ kJ} \end{aligned}$$

This represents the energy absorbed by the solution, which earns the first mark (M1).

ti84.instructions: Multiply mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

calculator.description: Calculate the heat change Q in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate moles of MgO
steps.content:
To find the molar enthalpy change, we must determine how many moles of $\text{MgO}$ reacted. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

The molar mass ($M$) of $\text{MgO}$ is $24.31 + 16.00 = 40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{MgO}) &= \frac{0.50\text{ g}}{40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n(\text{MgO}) &\approx 0.0124\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass of MgO by its molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of MgO.

steps.title: Determine molar enthalpy change
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate $\Delta H$ by dividing the heat change (in kJ) by the number of moles. Since the temperature of the solution increased, the reaction is exothermic, so $\Delta H$ must be negative.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta H &= -\frac{Q}{n} \\ \Delta H &= -\frac{1.68036\text{ kJ}}{0.012403\text{ mol}} \\ \Delta H &\approx -135\text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This final value earns the second mark (A1). Note that the markscheme accepts a range from $-135$ to $-140$ depending on how you rounded the intermediate steps.

gdc.instructions: Divide the kJ value by the moles calculated in the previous step.

ti84.instructions: Divide the heat (in kJ) by the moles, then apply the negative sign.

desmos.instructions: Perform the division and negate the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the enthalpy change in kJ/mol.

steps.title: Calculate temperature change
steps.content:
To find the heat change, we first need the change in temperature ($\Delta T$) for Trial 4. The initial temperature was $24.1^\circ\text{C}$ and the final temperature was $28.1^\circ\text{C}$.

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}} = 28.1^\circ\text{C} - 24.1^\circ\text{C} = 4.0\text{ K}$$

Note: A change in temperature of $1^\circ\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\text{ K}$.

steps.title: Determine heat change (Q)
steps.content:
Using the **Aqueous Solution Mass Approximation**, we assume the mass of the reacting mixture is solely that of the HCl solution. Given the volume is $100.0\text{ cm}^3$ and density is $1.00\text{ g cm}^{-3}$, the mass $m$ is $100.0\text{ g}$.

Using the formula for heat change:
$$Q = mc\Delta T$$
$$Q = 100.0\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1} \times 4.0\text{ K}$$
$$Q = 1672\text{ J} = 1.672\text{ kJ}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the values to find Q.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of heat change Q in Joules.

highlights.text: specific heat capacity of the solution is 4.18 J g−1 K−1

steps.title: Calculate moles of MgO
steps.content:
Next, we find the number of moles of $\text{MgO}$ used in Trial 4. The mass of $\text{MgO}$ is $0.50\text{ g}$ and its molar mass $M$ is $40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$
$$n(\text{MgO}) = \frac{0.50\text{ g}}{40.31\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0124\text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass of MgO by its molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of MgO.

steps.title: Calculate enthalpy change (ΔH)
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the molar enthalpy change. Since the temperature increased, the reaction is **exothermic**, meaning $\Delta H$ must be negative.

$$\Delta H = -\frac{Q}{n}$$
$$\Delta H = -\frac{1.672\text{ kJ}}{0.0124\text{ mol}} \approx -135\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This value represents the energy released per mole of $\text{MgO}$ reacted.

ti84.instructions: Divide the heat change in kJ by the number of moles.

desmos.instructions: Perform the division to find ΔH in kJ/mol.

calculator.description: Calculate the final enthalpy change ΔH.

parts.content:
Enthalpy of formation data for the reactants and products in the reaction is shown in the table below. 

| Substance | $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ ($\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\text{MgO(s)}$ | $-602$ |
| $\text{HCl(g)}$ | $-92.3$ |
| $\text{MgCl}_2\text{(s)}$ | $-642$ |
| $\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$ | $-286$ |

Using the data in the table, determine the $\Delta H$ for the reaction.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta H = \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ} \text{(products)} - \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ} \text{(reactants)}$
$\Delta H = [(-642) + (-286)] - [(-602) + 2(-92.3)]$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\Delta H = -928 - (-786.6) = -141\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>2</marks>

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To calculate the enthalpy change of reaction ($\Delta H$) using enthalpies of formation, we use the standard enthalpy formula found in your data booklet. This formula tells us to subtract the total enthalpy of the reactants from the total enthalpy of the products.

$$\Delta H^{\ominus} = \Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products}) - \Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

steps.title: Sum the products
steps.content:
First, let's identify the products from the chemical equation: $\text{MgCl}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. According to the stoichiometry, we have 1 mole of each. 

$$\sum \Delta H_f^{\circ} (\text{products}) = [1 \times (-642)] + [1 \times (-286)] = -928 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter -642 + -286 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of enthalpies for the products.

steps.title: Sum the reactants
steps.content:
Next, identify the reactants: $\text{MgO}$ and $2\text{HCl}$. Notice the coefficient of $2$ for $\text{HCl}$—we must multiply its formation value by $2$.

$$\sum \Delta H_f^{\circ} (\text{reactants}) = [1 \times (-602)] + [2 \times (-92.3)] = -786.6 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter -602 + 2 * -92.3 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of enthalpies for the reactants.

steps.title: Calculate final enthalpy
steps.content:
Now, subtract the sum of reactants from the sum of products to find the enthalpy change of reaction:

$$\Delta H = (-928) - (-786.6) = -141.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Rounding to the nearest whole number (as shown in the markscheme), we get **$-141 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$**.

ti84.instructions: Enter -928 - (-786.6) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Subtract the reactants from the products.

steps.title: Understand the Hess Cycle
steps.content:
To solve this using a **Hess’s Law Cycle**, we imagine a path where we start with the elements in their standard states at the bottom. 

Arrows point **up** from these elements to the reactants and the products because the enthalpy of formation ($
\Delta H_f^{\circ}$) is the energy change when 1 mole of a substance is formed from its elements. 

By following the arrows from the reactants back down to the elements (reverse formation) and then up to the products (forward formation), we can calculate the enthalpy of reaction ($
\Delta H$).

steps.title: Sum the products
steps.content:
First, we calculate the total enthalpy of formation for all products. According to the reaction:
$$\text{MgO(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

The products are 1 mole of $\text{MgCl}_2$ and 1 mole of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. 

$$\begin{aligned} \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ} (\text{products}) &= \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{MgCl}_2) + \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}) \\ &= (-642) + (-286) \\ &= -928 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sum the reactants
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the total enthalpy of formation for all reactants. Note that we must multiply by the stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation (2 for $\text{HCl}$).

$$\begin{aligned} \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ} (\text{reactants}) &= \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{MgO}) + 2 \times \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{HCl}) \\ &= (-602) + 2(-92.3) \\ &= -602 - 184.6 \\ &= -786.6 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate enthalpy of reaction
steps.content:
Using the formula from your Data Booklet, we find the difference between the products and reactants. 

$$\Delta H^{\ominus} = \Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products}) - \Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta H &= (-928) - (-786.6) \\ &= -928 + 786.6 \\ &= -141.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures as per standard IB practice (and the markscheme), we get **$-141$ kJ mol$^{-1}$**.

gdc.instructions: Input -928 - (-786.6) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter -928 - (-786.6) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final enthalpy change by subtracting the sum of reactants from the sum of products.

questions.specification:
A calorimeter was used to determine the molar enthalpy of solution ($\Delta H_{\text{sol}}$) for lithium chloride ($\text{LiCl}$) by dissolving it in water. The results of the experiment are shown below.

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mass of water | $50.00\text{ g}$ |
| Mass of $\text{LiCl}(s)$ | $1.06\text{ g}$ |
| Initial temperature of water | $20.20{^\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Final temperature of solution | $24.20{^\circ}\text{C}$ |

Using the specific heat capacity of water, $4.18\text{ J g}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$, and assuming the mass of the solution is $50.00\text{ g}$, what is the molar enthalpy of solution for $\text{LiCl}$? (Molar mass of $\text{LiCl} = 42.4\text{ g mol}^{-1}$)

questions.specification:
A student uses a simple calorimeter to determine the enthalpy of combustion of a liquid fuel. The results obtained are shown in the table below:

| Measurement | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mass of water in the calorimeter | $50.00\text{ g}$ |
| Temperature increase of the water | $20.00\text{ K}$ |
| Mass of fuel burned | $0.300\text{ g}$ |
| Molar mass of the fuel | $60.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ |

What is the enthalpy of combustion of the fuel, in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, assuming no heat loss to the surroundings?
(Specific heat capacity of water, $c = 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses calorimetry (q = mcΔT) to determine heat released by combustion and convert to molar enthalpy change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.3 Energy from fuels > R1.3.1 Combustion fuels
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.3.1 Combustion fuels
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Context is enthalpy of combustion of a fuel (energy from fuels/combustion).

options.markscheme: This value represents the total heat energy ($Q$) released in kJ ($4.18\text{ kJ}$), but it has not been divided by the number of moles of fuel burned.
This would be the result if the student incorrectly calculated the number of moles as $0.01$ (e.g., $0.6/60$) or failed to convert units correctly during the final step.
This is the correct numerical magnitude, but the sign is incorrect. Combustion is an exothermic process, so the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) must be negative.

1. Calculate heat energy ($Q$): $Q = mc\Delta T = 50.00\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1} \times 20.00\text{ K} = 4180\text{ J} = 4.18\text{ kJ}$.
2. Calculate moles of fuel ($n$): $n = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{0.300\text{ g}}{60.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.00500\text{ mol}$.
3. Calculate molar enthalpy ($\Delta H_c$): $\Delta H_c = -\frac{Q}{n} = -\frac{4.18\text{ kJ}}{0.00500\text{ mol}} = -836\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses heat released from combustion to predict temperature change of water via calorimetry (q = mcΔT), i.e., energy transfer in reactions.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.3.1 Combustion fuels
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves combustion of a fuel (pentane) and its molar enthalpy of combustion as an energy source.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.3 Molar mass
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires converting a given mass of pentane to moles using molar mass to apply ΔHc (kJ mol⁻¹).

parts.content: A $0.361\,\mathrm{g}$ sample of pentane ($C_5H_{12}$) is used in a spirit burner to heat $125.0\,\mathrm{g}$ of water. Calculate the expected temperature change of the water if the molar enthalpy of combustion for pentane is $-3509\,\mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$.
parts.markscheme:
$$q = n \times |\Delta H_c| = \frac{0.361\,\mathrm{g}}{72.17\,\mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}} \times 3509\,\mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}} = 17.545\,\mathrm{kJ} \text{ (or } 17545\,\mathrm{J})$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$\Delta T = \frac{q}{mc} = \frac{17545\,\mathrm{J}}{125.0\,\mathrm{g} \times 4.18\,\mathrm{J\,g^{-1}\,{}^\circ C^{-1}}} = 33.6\,{}^\circ\mathrm{C}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept answers in the range $33.5$ to $33.7\,{}^\circ\mathrm{C}$ depending on the molar mass used ($72.0$ to $72.2\,\mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$).</note>
<note>M1 can be awarded for calculating moles ($0.00500\,\mathrm{mol}$) or total energy released.</note>

questions.specification:
A student performs an experiment to determine the specific heat capacity of a metal. A cube of metal is placed in boiling water at a temperature of 100$^\circ$C. The metal cube is then placed into a calorimeter containing water. The data recorded is shown in the table below. 

| Property                          | Value     |
|----------------------------------|-----------|
| Mass of metal cube (g)           | 98.1      |
| Mass of water (g)                | 52.0      |
| Initial temperature of metal (°C)| 100.0     |
| Initial temperature of water (°C)| 25.0      |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The experiment is calorimetry-based: heat transfer between hot metal and water, using q=mcΔT to determine specific heat capacity and interpret heating curves; this falls under energy transfer/enthalpy measurement concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.5 Practical considerations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.5 Practical considerations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (e)–(f) evaluate insulating materials and sources of heat loss in a calorimetry setup, which aligns with practical considerations in calorimetry experiments.

parts.content:
Determine the highest temperature of the water from the diagram shown.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/475f8244-7983-4773-8690-e25a4bfd37f4)
parts.markscheme: $38.5^\circ C$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: $2940 \ J$ of heat is transferred from the metal block to the water. Use your answer to part (a) and the data in the table to calculate the specific heat capacity of the metal in $J\ g^{-1}\ ^\circ C^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
$Q=mcΔT$

$2940 = 98.1 × c × (100 − 38.5)$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$c =  2940 / 6033$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$c = 0.487 \ J \ g^{-1} \ ^\circ C^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: In a second experiment, $2940 \ J$ of heat is transferred from a block of aluminium to 52.0 g of water at an initial temperature of $25.0^\circ C$. Calculate the final temperature of the water.
parts.markscheme:
$Q = mcΔT$

$2940 = 52.0 × 4.18 × (ΔT)$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$ΔT = 13.5^\circ C $<marks> 1 mark </marks>

Final temperature of water $= 25.0 + 13.5 = 38.5^\circ C$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a1aceabf-b9dd-4ac1-80a8-74a4d2c39681)

Arrange the substances in order from highest specific heat capacity to lowest. Give a reason for your choice. No calculations are required.
parts.markscheme:
$A > B > C > D$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Substance A shows the lowest change in temperature when heat is added and substance D the least. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content:
A student conducts an experiment to determine the most effective insulating material for a calorimeter. Equal volumes of water at 80 oC were added to four beakers. Three of the beakers were covered with an insulating material and one without. The temperature of the water was measured every 10 minutes. The results are shown in the table below.

| Time (min) | No Insulation (°C) | Cotton Wool (°C) | Foam (°C) | Paper (°C) |
|------------|--------------------|------------------|-----------|------------|
| 0          | 80                 | 80               | 80        | 80         |
| 10         | 60                 | 70               | 76        | 65         |
| 20         | 45                 | 62               | 74        | 52         |
| 30         | 33                 | 56               | 71        | 41         |
| 40         | 30                 | 51               | 69        | 37         |
| 50         | 28                 | 47               | 65        | 29         |
| 60         | 25                 | 44               | 61        | 26         |

Which material shows the greatest temperature decrease after 30 seconds?
parts.markscheme: Paper  <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Based on the data in the table, which material is the best for insulating the calorimeter? Give a reason for your answer.
parts.markscheme:
Foam; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Foam shows the smallest temperature decrease after 60 minutes; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

questions.specification:
Which combination of $\Delta H_1$, $\Delta H_2$, and $\Delta H_3$ would give the enthalpy change for the combustion of propane?

$$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) + 5\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2(g) + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$

$$ \Delta H_1 \quad \text{C}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) $$

$$ \Delta H_2 \quad \text{H}_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$

$$ \Delta H_3 \quad 3\text{C}(s) + 4\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) $$

options.markscheme: This equation relates pressure–volume work per mole and is not the calorimetry relationship for molar enthalpy change.
This incorrectly multiplies the heat energy term ($q = mc\Delta T$) by the number of moles. Molar enthalpy change is found by dividing by $n$ (and using the correct sign convention).

In calorimetry, the heat change of the surroundings is $q_{\text{surr}} = mc\Delta T$. The reaction molar enthalpy change is $\Delta H = -\frac{q_{\text{surr}}}{n} = -\frac{mc\Delta T}{n}$ (convert J to kJ if required).
This incorrectly divides the heat energy term by pressure instead of the number of moles of limiting reactant.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the enthalpy of solution $(\Delta H_{sol})$ of ammonium nitrate $(\mathrm{NH}_4\mathrm{NO}_3)$ by dissolving it in water:

$$\mathrm{NH}_4\mathrm{NO}_3(\mathrm{s}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{NH}_4^+(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{NO}_3^-(\mathrm{aq})$$

A $8.01\text{ g}$ sample of $\mathrm{NH}_4\mathrm{NO}_3$ (molar mass $80.05\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) is added to $100.0\text{ g}$ of distilled water in a coffee-cup calorimeter. The temperature is recorded every minute. The solid is added at exactly $t = 3.0\text{ min}$ and the mixture is stirred continuously.

| Time (min) | Temperature ($^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.0 | 20.5 |
| 1.0 | 20.5 |
| 2.0 | 20.5 |
| 3.0 | Solid added |
| 4.0 | 15.4 |
| 5.0 | 15.8 |
| 6.0 | 16.2 |
| 7.0 | 16.6 |
| 8.0 | 17.0 |

Assume the specific heat capacity of the final solution is $4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ }^{\circ}\mathrm{C}^{-1}$ and that the mass of the solution is the sum of the mass of water and the mass of the salt.

parts.content: State whether the dissolution of ammonium nitrate is exothermic or endothermic, providing one piece of evidence from the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Endothermic. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The temperature of the water/surroundings decreases (from $20.5\text{ }^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $\approx 15\text{ }^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$), indicating energy was absorbed by the system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two assumptions made regarding the heat transfer or physical properties of the system when calculating the enthalpy change.
parts.markscheme:
Award **[1]** for any two valid assumptions:
- No heat is lost to or gained from the surroundings outside the calorimeter. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The specific heat capacity of the ammonium nitrate solution is the same as that of pure water ($4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The density of the solution is $1.00\text{ g mL}^{-1}$ (if volume was used instead of mass). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Heat is distributed uniformly throughout the solution by stirring. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Plot a graph of temperature against time from $t = 0$ to $t = 8\text{ minutes}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Axes correctly labeled with units (Time in min, Temperature in $^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- All points plotted correctly and two best-fit lines drawn (one for pre-mixing and one for post-mixing warming). <marks>1 mark</marks>

![Graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-9895a012-d6ee-4c21-9924-4930a87bc0f1-1771940959750/02b77db0-a6e9-4b7c-8e99-574060e3741d.jpeg)

parts.content: Explain why the temperature begins to rise after $4.0\text{ minutes}$ and describe how the graph is used to find the theoretical minimum temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- The temperature rises because the calorimeter is cooler than the room, so heat is absorbed from the surroundings. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The post-mixing linear portion of the graph is extrapolated back to the time of mixing ($t = 3.0\text{ min}$) to find the temperature that would have been reached if dissolution were instantaneous and no heat gain occurred. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the extrapolation method, determine the corrected temperature change $(\Delta T)$ and calculate the heat energy $(q)$, in $\text{J}$, absorbed by the $100.0\text{ g}$ of water alone.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine $\Delta T$:
Extrapolated $T$ at $t = 3$ is $15.4 - 0.4 = 15.0\text{ }^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.
$\Delta T = 20.5 - 15.0 = 5.5\text{ }^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate $q$:
$q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = 100.0 \times 4.18 \times 5.5 = 2299\text{ J}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the molar enthalpy of solution $(\Delta H_{sol})$ for ammonium nitrate in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, using the total mass of the solution $(108.01\text{ g})$ and your $\Delta T$ from part (e).
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate heat for total solution:
$q_{sol} = 108.01 \times 4.18 \times 5.5 = 2483\text{ J} = 2.483\text{ kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate moles of $\mathrm{NH}_4\mathrm{NO}_3$:
$n = \frac{8.01}{80.05} = 0.100\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

-  Calculate $\Delta H_{sol}$:
$\Delta H_{sol} = +\frac{2.483}{0.100} = +24.8\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Note: Award **[2]** if the positive sign is missing. Accept answers based on rounding, e.g., $24.8$ or $25$.*

parts.content: If the heat capacity of the calorimeter $(C_{cal})$ is $12.0\text{ J K}^{-1}$, calculate the additional heat energy absorbed from the calorimeter and determine the corrected $\Delta H_{sol}$ in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate heat from calorimeter:
$q_{cal} = C_{cal} \cdot \Delta T = 12.0 \times 5.5 = 66\text{ J}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Total heat change:
$q_{total} = 2483 + 66 = 2549\text{ J}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Corrected $\Delta H_{sol}$:
$\Delta H_{sol} = +\frac{2.549}{0.100} = +25.5\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Accept rounding to $25.5$.*

questions.specification:
A student determines the molar enthalpy of solution for a salt by dissolving a sample of the solid in a known mass of water using a coffee-cup calorimeter. The data collected are shown in the table below:

| Measurement | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mass of water in the calorimeter | $m_{\text{w}}$ |
| Mass of the solid salt added | $m_{\text{s}}$ |
| Molar mass of the salt | $M_{\text{r}}$ |
| Specific heat capacity of the resulting solution | $c$ |
| Measured change in temperature | $\Delta T$ |

Assuming the heat capacity of the calorimeter is negligible and the mass of the final solution is equal to the sum of the masses of the salt and water, which expression represents the molar enthalpy of solution, $\Delta H_{\text{sol}}$, in $\text{J mol}^{-1}$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is a coffee-cup calorimetry setup requiring use of q = mcΔT and relating heat transfer to an enthalpy change of solution per mole.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.5 Practical considerations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.5 Practical considerations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It explicitly uses calorimetry assumptions (negligible calorimeter heat capacity, solution mass equals sum of masses), which are practical considerations in measuring enthalpy changes.

options.markscheme: Incorrect mass. This expression only uses the mass of the water ($m_{\text{w}}$) for the heat calculation ($q = mc\Delta T$), neglecting the mass of the solute ($m_{\text{s}}$) that contributes to the total mass of the solution.

Correct. The total heat absorbed or released is $q = (m_{\text{w}} + m_{\text{s}}) \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$. The number of moles of salt is $n = \frac{m_{\text{s}}}{M_{\text{r}}}$. The molar enthalpy is $\Delta H = \frac{q}{n} = \frac{(m_{\text{w}} + m_{\text{s}}) \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{\frac{m_{\text{s}}}{M_{\text{r}}}}$, which simplifies to $\frac{M_{\text{r}} \cdot c \cdot \Delta T \cdot (m_{\text{w}} + m_{\text{s}})}{m_{\text{s}}}$.
Incorrect mole calculation. This expression incorrectly divides by the molar mass ($M_{\text{r}}$) again instead of multiplying by the reciprocal of the moles ($n = m_{\text{s}}/M_{\text{r}}$).
Incorrect formula structure. This expression does not correctly relate the heat change to the molar quantity and is dimensionally inconsistent with $\text{J mol}^{-1}$.

parts.content: Determine the limiting reactant showing your working.
parts.markscheme:
$n(\text{CuSO}_4) = 0.0500\text{ dm}^3 \times 0.300\text{ mol dm}^{-3} = 0.0150\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$n(\text{Zn}) = \frac{1.50\text{ g}}{65.38\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0229\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$\text{CuSO}_4$ is the limiting reactant because $0.0150 < 0.0229$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not award the second mark if mole calculation for zinc is not shown.</note>

parts.content: The mass of copper obtained experimentally was $0.785\text{ g}$. Calculate the percentage yield of copper.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

$$\text{Theoretical mass of Cu} = 0.0150\text{ mol} \times 63.55\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.953\text{ g}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$\text{Percentage yield} = \frac{0.785\text{ g}}{0.953\text{ g}} \times 100\% = 82.4\%$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Method #2

$$\text{Experimental mol of Cu} = \frac{0.785\text{ g}}{63.55\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0124\text{ mol}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$\text{Percentage yield} = \frac{0.0124\text{ mol}}{0.0150\text{ mol}} \times 100\% = 82.7\%$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept answers in the range 82–83% depending on intermediate rounding.</note>

parts.content:
The reaction was carried out in a calorimeter. The maximum temperature rise of the solution was $12.4\text{ }^\circ\text{C}$.

Calculate the enthalpy change, $\Delta H$, of the reaction, in kJ, assuming that all the heat released was absorbed by the solution.
parts.markscheme:
$q = 50.0\text{ g} \times 4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1} \times 12.4\text{ K} = 2.59 \times 10^3\text{ J}$ OR $2.59\text{ kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$\Delta H \text{ per mol of } \text{CuSO}_4 = -\frac{2.59\text{ kJ}}{0.0150\text{ mol}} = -173\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$\Delta H = -173\text{ kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks for -173 kJ. Award 2 marks for 173 kJ (missing negative sign).</note>

questions.specification: What is the main reason that bomb calorimeters provide more accurate values for the enthalpy of combustion than simple calorimeters?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.5 Practical considerations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.5 Practical considerations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares bomb vs simple calorimeters and asks why bomb calorimeters give more accurate enthalpy of combustion values—this is about experimental/practical sources of error such as minimizing heat loss and ensuring complete combustion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.1 Energy transfer in chemical reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Enthalpy of combustion determination is fundamentally about measuring energy transfer as heat during a reaction; the accuracy discussion relates to how that heat transfer is captured.

options.markscheme: While bomb calorimeters are well-insulated (often with a water jacket), they do not typically use vacuum insulation like a Dewar flask. This is not the primary reason for their accuracy in combustion measurements.

options.content: They measure enthalpy under constant pressure
options.markscheme: Bomb calorimeters measure heat at constant volume, so the energy change measured is related to $\Delta U$. The enthalpy change $\Delta H$ is then calculated from this result. Simple calorimeters operate at (approximately) constant pressure.

options.content: They allow for 100% heat transfer to water
options.markscheme: No calorimeter allows for 100% heat transfer as some heat will always be absorbed by the calorimeter components themselves (the bomb, stirrer, etc.), though this is accounted for in the heat capacity of the calorimeter.

options.content: They minimize heat loss and ensure complete combustion
options.markscheme: Bomb calorimeters use high-pressure oxygen to ensure complete combustion of the sample and are heavily insulated with a water jacket to minimize heat loss to the surroundings, leading to more accurate results.

questions.specification:
A student uses a calorimetry setup to determine the enthalpy of combustion of propan-1-ol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). The experimental data collected is shown in the table below:

| Measured Parameter | Recorded Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mass of water in copper calorimeter | $150.00 \text{ g}$ |
| Initial temperature of water | $20.2^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Final temperature of water | $44.7^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Initial mass of spirit burner + propan-1-ol | $124.65 \text{ g}$ |
| Final mass of spirit burner + propan-1-ol | $123.92 \text{ g}$ |

Specific heat capacity of water, $c = 4.18 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$.
Molar mass of propan-1-ol = $60.11 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-a24a3e58-82a1-4bf1-8084-c00f0dfeaa4f-1771935780608/766a59ea-ef7a-40ee-ba51-2908d53702f1.jpeg)

parts.content: Calculate the heat energy ($q$), in $\text{kJ}$, absorbed by the water during the experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- $\Delta T = 44.7 - 20.2 = 24.5 \text{ K}$ (or $^\circ\text{C}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $q = mc\Delta T = 150.00 \times 4.18 \times 24.5 = 15361.5 \text{ J} = 15.36 \text{ kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using your answer from part (a), calculate the experimental enthalpy of combustion ($\Delta H_c$) of propan-1-ol in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- $n(\text{propan-1-ol}) = \frac{124.65 - 123.92}{60.11} = 0.01214 \text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta H_c = -\frac{15.36}{0.01214} = -1265 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ (accept $-1264$ to $-1266$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: The literature value for the enthalpy of combustion of propan-1-ol is $-2021 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. State two experimental reasons why the value calculated in part (b) is less exothermic than the literature value.
parts.markscheme:
- Significant heat loss to the surroundings (air/calorimeter). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Incomplete combustion of the fuel (formation of soot/carbon monoxide). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- *Other valid points: evaporation of alcohol from wick, heat capacity of copper can ignored.*

parts.content:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the hydration of ethene to ethanol using the standard enthalpies of formation provided:

$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l)$

- $\Delta H_f [\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(g)] = +52 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $\Delta H_f [\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)] = -242 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $\Delta H_f [\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l)] = -278 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
parts.markscheme:
- $\Delta H = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta H = (-278) - [52 + (-242)] = -88 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The enthalpy of combustion of methane can be estimated using average bond enthalpies. Given the data below, calculate the enthalpy change for: $\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$

| Bond | Average Bond Enthalpy ($\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| C-H | 413 |
| O=O | 498 |
| C=O | 805 |
| O-H | 464 |
parts.markscheme:
- Bonds broken $= 4(413) + 2(498) = 2648 \text{ kJ}$; Bonds formed $= 2(805) + 4(464) = 3466 \text{ kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta H = 2648 - 3466 = -818 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State what is meant by the term activation energy, and explain how it is represented on the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- Activation energy is the minimum energy required for the reactants to form the activated complex (transition state). <marks>1</marks>
- It is represented by the vertical distance from the reactants to the peak of the curve ($E_a = 64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$). <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define activation energy
steps.content:
In chemistry, **activation energy** ($E_a$) is defined as the minimum energy that colliding particles must possess in order for a chemical reaction to occur. 

Another way to describe it is the energy required for the reactants to reach the **transition state** (or activated complex), which is the highest energy point along the reaction coordinate. This concept is a key part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify diagram representation
steps.content:
On an energy profile diagram, the activation energy is represented by the **vertical distance** measured from the enthalpy level of the **reactants** to the **peak of the curve**.

Looking at the provided diagram for the combustion of methane:
- The reactants ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$) are at the starting horizontal line.
- The peak represents the transition state.
- The difference between these two points is the energy barrier the molecules must overcome, which is given as:

$$E_a = 64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you need to provide both the theoretical definition and the visual identification:

1.  **Definition**: Minimum energy required for a successful collision or to form the transition state (1 mark).
2.  **Representation**: The vertical distance from the reactants to the peak of the curve (1 mark).

parts.content: Deduce whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic and justify your answer using the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- The reaction is exothermic. <marks>1</marks>
- The products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating energy is released. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactant and product levels
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the horizontal lines on the energy profile diagram. The line on the left represents the energy level of the **reactants** ($CH_4 + 2O_2$), and the line on the right represents the energy level of the **products** ($CO_2 + 2H_2O$).

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in your IB syllabus.

highlights.text: The energy profile diagram below shows the progress of the reaction

steps.title: Compare the energy levels
steps.content:
By looking at the vertical 'Energy' axis, we can visually compare the heights of these two lines. 

Notice that the line for the **products** is physically **lower** than the line for the **reactants**. This indicates that the chemical systems' potential energy has decreased during the reaction.

highlights.text: The products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating energy is released.

steps.title: Determine the reaction type
steps.content:
Because the products have less energy than the reactants, the 'missing' energy must have been released to the surroundings (usually as heat). 

When a reaction releases energy to its surroundings and the energy level of the system drops, we classify it as **exothermic**.

steps.title: Examine the energy levels
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the energy profile diagram provided. This diagram shows the relative potential energy of the substances involved as the reaction progresses from start to finish.

On the left side of the graph, we see the energy level for the **reactants** ($CH_4 + 2O_2$). On the right side, we see the energy level for the **products** ($CO_2 + 2H_2O$).

highlights.text: justify your answer using the diagram

steps.title: Compare products and reactants
steps.content:
By observing the vertical axis (Potential Energy), we can see that the products are at a **lower energy level** than the reactants. 

In chemistry, the enthalpy change ($ΔH$) is defined as the difference between the enthalpy of the products and the enthalpy of the reactants:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$ 

This concept is a core part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Analyze the enthalpy sign
steps.content:
Since the products have less energy than the reactants ($H_{\text{products}} < H_{\text{reactants}}$), the value of $\Delta H$ must be **negative** (less than zero).

$$\Delta H < 0$$

This means that the system has "lost" energy to the surroundings in the form of heat during the transition from reactants to products.

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
A reaction where energy is released and $\Delta H$ is negative is defined as an **exothermic** reaction. 

In summary:
1. The reaction is **exothermic**.
2. This is because the products are at a **lower energy level** than the reactants, indicating that energy has been released to the surroundings.

highlights.text: Deduce whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic

parts.content: The enthalpy change of the reaction is $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. On the same diagram, sketch and label this enthalpy change.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch a vertical arrow from reactants to products, labeled $\Delta H = -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. <marks>1</marks>
- Arrow should point downward to show exothermic nature. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactant and product levels
steps.content:
In an energy profile diagram, the horizontal lines represent the potential energy of the substances involved. The level on the left represents the **reactants** ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$), and the level on the right represents the **products** ($\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

To represent the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$), we look at the vertical difference between these two specific levels.

steps.title: Determine the arrow direction
steps.content:
The question states that the enthalpy change is $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. Because the value is negative, the reaction is **exothermic**, meaning energy is released to the surroundings and the products have lower potential energy than the reactants.

In diagrammatic terms, this means our arrow must point **downwards**, starting from the reactant level and ending at the product level.

highlights.text: enthalpy change of the reaction is -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}
Arrow should point downward to show exothermic nature.

steps.title: Sketch and label the change
steps.content:
Now, draw a vertical line between the two horizontal levels. 

1. Start the arrow at the energy level of the reactants.
2. Draw it straight down to the level of the products.
3. Add an arrowhead pointing down.
4. Label the arrow clearly as $\Delta H = -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.

Note: The activation energy ($E_a$) is the "hill" from the reactants to the peak, but $\Delta H$ only measures the overall change from start to finish.

highlights.text: Sketch a vertical arrow from reactants to products, labeled \Delta H = -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}.

parts.content: Suggest how the presence of a catalyst affects the reaction mechanism and energy profile.
parts.markscheme:
- A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy. <marks>1</marks>
- The peak of the curve would be lower in the presence of a catalyst. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction mechanism change
steps.content:
A catalyst works by changing the way a reaction happens at a molecular level. Instead of following the original route, it provides an **alternative reaction mechanism** (or pathway) for the reactants to turn into products. This is the first key point required for your marks.

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

highlights.text: Suggest how the presence of a catalyst affects the reaction mechanism

steps.title: Explain activation energy reduction
steps.content:
The most important feature of this alternative pathway is that it has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$). 

In the original reaction, molecules needed at least $64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ of energy to react. With a catalyst, the new "pathway" requires less energy to overcome the initial barrier, allowing more particles to have enough energy to react at a given temperature.

steps.title: Describe the energy profile change
steps.content:
On an energy profile diagram, the activation energy is represented by the distance from the reactants to the top of the curve (the peak). 

Because the catalyst lowers the activation energy, the **peak of the curve would be lower** than the original. Note that the energy of the reactants and products (and thus the overall $\Delta H$) remains exactly the same; only the "hill" in the middle changes.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks for this question, ensure your answer includes these two specific points:
1. The catalyst provides an **alternative pathway/mechanism** with a **lower activation energy**.
2. The **peak of the curve** on the energy profile diagram is **lower**.

parts.content: Use the collision theory to explain how temperature affects the rate of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Increasing temperature increases the kinetic energy of particles. <marks>1</marks>
- More particles have energy $\ge E_a$, so more successful collisions per second, increasing the rate. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Temperature and Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
To understand how temperature affects the rate, we first look at the particles themselves. According to the Kinetic Molecular Theory (which you can review in **Topic S1.1.3**), temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. 

When we increase the temperature of the methane and oxygen gas mixture, the particles gain energy and move faster. This directly increases their **average kinetic energy**.

steps.title: Meeting the Activation Energy
steps.content:
For a collision between methane and oxygen to actually result in a reaction (a "successful collision"), the particles must collide with a minimum amount of energy. This is called the **activation energy** ($E_a$).

In this specific reaction, the particles need at least $64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ to break the existing bonds and start the reaction.

steps.title: Frequency of Successful Collisions
steps.content:
At a higher temperature, not only are there more frequent collisions because particles move faster, but more importantly, a **higher proportion** of these collisions have energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E \ge E_a$). 

Because more particles meet this energy threshold, there is an increase in the **frequency of successful collisions** (number of successful collisions per second).

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The overall rate of reaction depends on how often successful collisions occur. Since the frequency of successful collisions has increased due to the higher temperature, the **rate of reaction increases**.

To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention:
1. The increase in **kinetic energy**.
2. More particles having **energy $\ge E_a$**, leading to more **successful collisions per unit time**.

steps.title: Link temperature to energy
steps.content:
According to the kinetic molecular theory, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. When the temperature of the reaction mixture increases, the particles move faster and their average kinetic energy increases.

This concept is a prerequisite from **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Apply Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
steps.content:
A Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve shows the distribution of kinetic energies of particles at a specific temperature. When temperature increases:
1. The peak of the curve shifts to the right (higher energy).
2. The curve flattens (the peak is lower).

This shift means that the overall distribution of particles is moved toward higher energy levels.

steps.title: Evaluate activation energy threshold
steps.content:
The question identifies a specific threshold for this reaction: the activation energy. 

$$\text{activation energy of } 64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

While the value of $E_a$ remains constant at $64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, the shift in the Maxwell-Boltzmann curve means that a significantly larger area under the curve now lies to the right of this $E_a$ value.

steps.title: Relate to collision frequency
steps.content:
Since a greater proportion of particles now possess energy $\ge E_a$, there is a higher frequency of successful collisions (collisions with enough energy to overcome the energy barrier). 

As more successful collisions occur per unit of time, the overall rate of the combustion reaction increases. This aligns with the requirements of the markscheme:
- **Mark 1:** Increasing temperature increases the kinetic energy of particles.
- **Mark 2:** More particles have energy $\ge E_a$, leading to more successful collisions per second.

parts.content: Determine the activation energy for the reverse reaction. Show your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
- Reverse reaction: $\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$ <marks>1</marks>
- $E_a(\text{reverse}) = E_a(\text{forward}) + |\Delta H| = 64 + 802 = 866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reverse reaction
steps.content:
To find the activation energy for the reverse reaction, we first need to define what that reaction is. In a reverse reaction, the products of the original equation become the reactants.

The reverse reaction is:
$$\text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g)$$ 

This is a critical first step as it sets the direction for our energy calculations, earning the first mark.

steps.title: Extract values from graph
steps.content:
Next, we identify the key energy values from the provided energy profile diagram and the question text:

1.  **Forward Activation Energy ($E_a(\text{forward})$):** Given as $64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. This represents the energy barrier starting from the reactants.
2.  **Enthalpy Change ($\Delta H$):** Looking at the diagram, the products are at a lower energy level than the reactants. The difference is $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. The negative sign indicates an exothermic reaction.

steps.title: Apply energy relationship
steps.content:
We can now use the algebraic relationship between enthalpy change and activation energies:

$$\Delta H = E_a(\text{forward}) - E_a(\text{reverse})$$

By rearranging this formula to solve for the reverse activation energy, we get:

$$E_a(\text{reverse}) = E_a(\text{forward}) - \Delta H$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 - (-802) \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 + 802 \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression to add the energies together.

ti84.instructions: Enter the forward activation energy (64) and add the absolute value of the enthalpy change (802).

desmos.instructions: Type the simple addition into the calculator cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the forward activation energy and the magnitude of the enthalpy change.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The activation energy for the reverse reaction is **$866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$**.

In an energy profile, this represents the total vertical distance from the product level ($-802$) all the way up to the transition state peak ($+64$).

steps.title: Identify the reverse reaction
steps.content:
To find the activation energy for the reverse reaction, we first need to identify what that reaction is. In a reverse reaction, the products of the original equation become the reactants. 

The reverse reaction is:
$$\text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g)$$ 

This is a key step in IB Chemistry, as it sets the context for where we are starting on our energy diagram.

steps.title: Identify energy levels
steps.content:
Using the **Energy Profile Diagram Analysis** approach, we look at the vertical positions on the graph:

1.  **Transition State (Peak):** The peak is the highest energy point. The question states the activation energy ($E_a$) for the forward reaction is $64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, which means the peak is $64$ units above the reactants.
2.  **Products:** The diagram shows the products are at an energy level of $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ relative to the reactants.

For the reverse reaction, we start at the product level and must reach the transition state.

steps.title: Calculate the vertical distance
steps.content:
The activation energy for the reverse reaction ($E_a(\text{reverse})$) is the total vertical distance from the product energy level to the transition state peak.

$$\begin{aligned} E_a(\text{reverse}) &= \text{Energy of Peak} - \text{Energy of Products} \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 - (-802) \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 + 802 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation accounts for climbing the $802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ from the products back up to the reactant level, plus the additional $64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ to reach the peak.

ti84.instructions: Enter 64 + 802 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the forward activation energy and the magnitude of the enthalpy change.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Summing these values gives us the total energy required for the reverse process:

$$E_a(\text{reverse}) = 866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for identifying the reverse reaction and one mark for the correct calculation and final value.

questions.specification:
A technician performs a calorimetry experiment to determine the enthalpy of neutralization for the reaction between potassium hydroxide ($KOH$) and nitric acid ($HNO_3$). The data collected during the experiment is summarized in the table below.

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Volume of $1.00 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ $KOH(aq)$ | $50.0 \text{ cm}^3$ |
| Volume of $1.00 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ $HNO_3(aq)$ | $50.0 \text{ cm}^3$ |
| Initial temperature of both solutions | $19.5 ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Maximum temperature reached by the mixture | $26.2 ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Density of the resulting solution | $1.02 \text{ g cm}^{-3}$ |
| Specific heat capacity of the solution | $4.05 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$ |

parts.content: Using the provided data, calculate the molar enthalpy of neutralization for this reaction in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. Give your answer to three significant figures.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the total mass of the solution mixture:
$$m = (50.0 \text{ cm}^3 + 50.0 \text{ cm}^3) \times 1.02 \text{ g cm}^{-3} = 102 \text{ g}$$
<marks> 1 mark </marks>

Calculate the temperature change of the mixture:
$$\Delta T = 26.2 ^\circ\text{C} - 19.5 ^\circ\text{C} = 6.7 \text{ K}$$
<marks> 1 mark </marks>

Calculate the heat energy released ($q$) using $q = mc\Delta T$:
$$q = 102 \text{ g} \times 4.05 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1} \times 6.7 \text{ K} = 2767.77 \text{ J}$$
<marks> 1 mark </marks>

Calculate the amount (in moles) of water formed and the molar enthalpy change:
$$n(H_2O) = c \times V = 1.00 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \times 0.0500 \text{ dm}^3 = 0.0500 \text{ mol}$$
$$\Delta H = -\frac{q}{n} = -\frac{2767.77 \text{ J}}{0.0500 \text{ mol}} = -55355.4 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -55.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$
<marks> 1 mark </marks>

<note>Apply ECF if $q$ is calculated correctly based on an incorrect mass. Accept answers in the range -55.3 to -55.5 depending on intermediate rounding. Deduct 1 mark if the negative sign is omitted from the final answer.</note>

questions.specification: A student carries out an experiment to determine the change in enthalpy when solid urea, $\text{CO}(\text{NH}_2)_2$, dissolves in water.
